ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
Melee Actions
A melee action represents a combatant’s focus on me-
lee, whether they are taking a combat posture, bracing
for attack, or actually engaged in melee. A character
may take a melee action regardless of whether they
are currently in melee, and doing so may be necessary
if the combatant wants to parry a foe who is charging
towards them or to strike a foe who is about to go run-
ning past.
The target of the melee action need not be declared
until the combatant chooses to resolve the action, at
which point the target must be within melee range. A
```
combatant may parry any number of combatants (Sec-
```
```
tion 9.6) as long as his last Action Point was spent on
```
melee.
Faster Rounds
If a faster paced round is desired, the number of Ac-
tion Phases may be reduced to 2, or even 1. With 2
Action Phases, each combatant spends up to 2 AP on
actions.
With 1 Action Phase, each combatant takes all of
their actions and movement at once. Note that, by
design, the pace penalty for moving while attacking
is normally the same as the penalty for expending
AP to move the same amount. For example, a Creep
```
pace (moving half your BMR over the course of the
```
```
round) incurs no pace penalty and requires no AP,
```
```
while Jogging (moving twice your BMR) incurs a
```
-50 pace penalty or takes 2 AP, resulting in a 2 AP
attack for the same -50 penalty. So, in many cases
it will be easier to treat moving your half BMR as a
free action, with each full increment of your BMR
costing one AP.
With fewer Action Phases, more conflicts are bound
to happen as each combatant is allowed to do more
at a single time. If conflicts arise use the Opposed
```
Maneuvers rule (Section 8.2) to determine the out-
```
come. Also, assume that combatants who have not
yet acted may freely declare parry, dodge, and other
defenses including instantaneous spells at the time
they are attacked.
Unlike other optional rules, this rule may be used off
and on as needed. Some combats, or even individual
rounds, may not have as much chance for conflict
```
(long distance missile combat, or initial rounds when
```
```
two groups are far apart and closing with each other).
```
In these cases rounds may be sped up by having the
combatants spend more Action Points at once. With
a larger number of combatants within close quar-
ters, using 4 Action Phases will provide the greatest
amount of fluidity.
Conditional Actions
If the maximum Action Points have been spent on an
action, but the combatant does not wish to resolve it,
he may continue to hold it until some declared condi-
tion occurs. The action is resolved when the triggered
```
condition occurs (often on someone else’s initiative).
```
```
Example: Anika has been hastened, giving her 6 AP
```
this round, and she rolls an initiative of 18. On the
first Action Phase, she steps around a corner for
cover against some archers and spends her first
AP on a melee attack. On the second Action Phase,
she spends a second AP on a melee attack. She
could now attack immediately at a penalty, but none
of her foes come around the corner. On the third
Action Phase, she spends two AP completing an
```
attack (since she is Hasted) and the action could be
```
complete, but still no targets. On the fourth Action
Phase, she continues to hold this action because
they’ll come around the corner “any time now.”
A held action counts as concentration, thereby dou-
bling the Action Point requirements of all other ac-
tions. Thus the combatant may move and perform
other actions, as long as he is still in a position to see
the triggering event and complete the previously held
action. Some held actions may, by their nature, pre-
```
vent other actions from being performed (e.g., holding
```
a bow in firing position would prevent the combat-
```
ant from doing anything else requiring their hands).
```
Concentration cannot be maintained forever and will
```
eventually cause fatigue (Section 5.5).
```
```
Example: Brundel draws an arrow on the first Action
```
```
Phase (1 AP). He would like to shoot the first per-
```
son to step through the door he was told to guard.
He spends the next three Action Phases on this
action, but has not actually fired yet. After the first
round of waiting, Brundel can declare and resolve
```
firing an arrow at no penalty (normally 3 AP) at
```
anyone coming through the door on their initiative
as if it were a 0 AP action. While waiting, he may
take other actions, as long as he keeps concentrat-
ing on the held action.
Open Rounds
Some GMs may allow actions to cross over into the
next round, thereby allowing Action Points spent in
a previous round to count toward the total needed to
resolve the action.
```
Example: Brundel begins the round by equipping his
```
```
bow (1 AP) then getting and nocking an arrow (1
```
```
AP). He wants to fire without penalty which requires
```
3 AP. He begins aiming and firing, but his attack is
not resolved until the next round when he spends an
additional Action Point in the first Action Phase.161
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
Upkeep phase
The last step in the round is to account for ongoing
effects. During upkeep, the players and the GM should
perform the following for all combatants:
•Subtract hits due to bleeding.
•Reduce the remaining duration of all spells or other
non-stun, timed effects by 1 round. For spell ef-
fects on combatants, only reduce the duration after
the first round if the combatant had spent less than
2 AP before being affected.
•Reduce stun effects 1 round, starting with the most
```
severe effect (Stun [-75], Stun [-50], then Stun
```
```
[-25]) if the combatant had spent less than 2 AP
```
before being stunned.
•Remove any staggered effect if the combatant has
already lost their 1 AP.
```
•Advance any environmental or other effects (e.g., a
```
```
rising tide, or a spreading fire).
```

Arms LawChapter 9:Chapter 9:
AttacksAttacks
163
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
Once the combatants have readied their weapons,
come into contact with their foes, and expended their
action points, it is time to determine the outcome of
their attacks.
Attacks are resolved like other maneuvers: a d100
```
open-ended (upward only) roll is made and the appro-
```
priate attack skill bonus, called the Offensive Bonus
```
(OB), is added. The target’s Defensive Bonus (DB) is
```
then subtracted from the total roll. There are a variety
of modifiers to both the attacker’s OB and the defend-
er’s DB depending on the nature of the attack and the
actions of the defender.
The result is determined by consulting the appropriate
weapon or attack table. Each attack table, determined
by the weapon used, has one column for each Armor
```
Type (AT). Look up the final attack roll for the appro-
```
priate AT. The result will indicate either a miss or the
```
number of hits delivered (e.g., 12), or the number of
```
```
hits and a critical (e.g., 18AS). The critical result con-
```
sists of two letters, with the first letter indicating one
```
of 10 levels of severity (A through J) and the second
```
```
letter indicating the type of critical delivered (e.g., K
```
```
= Krush, S = Slash).
```
fUmbling
Each type of attack or weapon is given a fumble num-
ber which represents the chance of mishandling the
```
attack. If the unmodified attack roll (i.e., the actual
```
```
number rolled, no skill bonuses, etc.) is equal to or
```
less than the weapon’s fumble number, a roll is imme-
```
diately made on the appropriate fumble table (Chapter
```
```
10). Skilled combatants can reduce the chance of fum-
```
bling. For every 5 ranks the character has in the attack
skill being used, the fumble number is reduced by 1,
to a minimum of 1.
```
Example: Volosha is wielding a heavy flail (fumble
```
```
of 10), with which she has 6 ranks. Since she has at
```
least 5 ranks she reduces the fumble by 1, to an un-
```
modified 9 or less. Unfortunately it does not matter;
```
Volosha rolls a 4 and must immediately roll on the
appropriate fumble table.
absolUte hit!
If a combatant’s total attack roll is over 175 then he
gets a bonus to the critical roll of +1 for every 5 above
175, rounding up. This stacks with any other critical
```
modifier (e.g., Slaying attacks and Ambush).
```
```
Example: Ghirin, confronted by the merciless Lord
```
Golan and his guards, in a last act of desperation,
grabs a nearby pole arm and swings wildly at Go-
```
lan (who is wearing AT 10, full plate armor). Ghirin
```
rolls a 100, followed by a 98, followed by an 89, for
a total of 287. His unskilled melee skill of -25, stat
bonus of -5, and Golan’s DB of 23 brings the total
attack to 234. Ghirin gleefully rolls his E puncture
```
critical: an 87 + 12 bonus (234 is 59 points over
```
```
175, adding +12 to the critical roll) results in a 99.
```
Lord Golan is no more.
9.1. Melee
& Ranged Attacks
A melee attack is an offensive strike against an oppo-
nent with, optionally, some offensive capability sac-
```
rificed for defense (parrying). The melee action rep-
```
resents an exchange of blows which spans part or all
of the round, not merely a single swing of a weapon.
Each combatant is looking for the opportunity to land
a solid blow, and the result of the attack roll describes
the outcome of the best opportunity during the course
of the action. Such attacks may be made using a melee
weapon, natural weapons such as claws, or by an un-
armed combatant with their bare hands or feet.
A ranged attack is usually a single shot, and cannot
be used to parry, nor can it be parried. Ranged attacks
take less activity than melee attacks, although the
combatant may still wish to take some time to aim.
Most ranged attacks require some additional time to
```
draw a weapon (e.g., to ready a throwing dagger),
```
```
ready a new missile (e.g., to load a bow), or load and
```
```
prepare a weapon (e.g., to draw and load a crossbow).
```
9.2. Directed Spells
Spells that resolve an attack roll vs. a single target are
```
called directed spells (e.g., elemental bolts, phantasms
```
```
that make attacks against a foe). This can include
```
supernatural or innate ranged attacks. Breath attacks
may be treated as bolts if directed against a single op-
ponent, or as balls or cones if used as an area attack.
Bolts can be fumbled on an unmodified roll of 01-02
```
(unlike other attacks, the fumble range is not reduced
```
```
by the caster’s skill ranks). If the bolt is produced by a
```
spell, the caster has both a chance to fail the spell and
a chance to fumble the bolt.
•Elemental Bolts: All types of elemental bolts, most
commonly the result of spells. Water, Shock, Fire,
Ice, Lightning.
•Illusionary Attacks: Some types of Illusions are
able to make attacks against foes, in which case
they use this skill as an Offensive Bonus.
•Hurling: Telekinetic force can be used to launch
objects as magically-driven weapons.
9. At tacks9. At tacks
164164
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
9.3. Area Attacks
```
Area effect attacks (e.g., elemental balls, breath weap-
```
```
ons) are not treated like other attacks, and there are
```
no corresponding skills since aiming is of little impor-
tance in harming individual targets, it being more of
an issue of hitting the right mass or area of targets. In-
stead, the only modifier to the attack roll is the level of
ranks in the originating spell list or, if an innate ability,
the level of the creature. Defensive Bonus from Quick-
ness applies as the combatants are assumed to have a
split second to shield themselves from the attack, but
```
dodging (Section 9.6) does not. Shields and cover only
```
apply if they are between the center point of the attack
```
(or the origin for cone attacks) and the defender.
```
For ball attacks, one target may be specified as the
```
primary target (and center point), who will be subject
```
to an attack that is +1 size greater than normal. For
cone attacks, anyone along the centerline is subject to
a +1 size attack. For all area effect attacks, targets that
straddle an edge of an area effect receive the attack at
-1 size.
Unless otherwise specified, a cone is 1' wide at the
origin and spreads to a width of 5' per 10' of range. For
example, a 30' long cone reaches a maximum width
of 15'.
Area effect spells may be fumbled on an unmodified
```
roll of 01-02; oil flasks and similar improvised area
```
effect weapons fumble on 01-04.
In some cases, most notably with thrown weapons that
```
have an area effect (e.g., oil flasks, holy water, magical
```
```
fire ball globes), it may be important to determine how
```
accurately the center point was placed. The GM should
apply a difficulty based on the precision desired. A
Percentage Maneuver is then made using the appro-
```
priate skill (generally Ranged Weapon: Thrown). A
```
result of 100 or more means
the missile was placed where
desired. Anything less than
a 100% Success indicates it
was off the target. Determine
half the distance to the target
and take a percentage equal
to 100 – the percentage of
success. Randomize the di-
```
rection by using a d6 (hex
```
```
facings), d8 (square grid) or
```
```
a d12 (clock-face).
```
```
Example: Caittina throws
```
an oil lamp 50' at an on-
coming group of six thugs
that are spread out over a
20' diameter. Caittina has
a +50 bonus in Thrown skill. The GM determines a
lamp of this size will inflict an A critical in a 20 foot
```
radius, with a +1 size (B critical) on the center tar-
```
get if one is aimed for. Caittina declines to throw it
at a specific thug, and instead tries to catch them all
in the radius, so makes a Medium Absolute Maneu-
ver using her skill. Unfortunately she rolls a final
result of a 40%. The Lamp/Bomb is off target. Half
the distance of 50' is 25, and she missed by 100-40
= 60% of 25' = 15'. The GM decides to use clock
face style directions with 12 being due behind and
6 being directly ahead of the thugs. Caittina rolls a
d12 and gets a 6… the lamp bomb has landed 15'
short of the thugs 35' away from her and in front
```
of most of the thugs (she still manages to hit two of
```
```
them in front for an A critical). At least the whole
```
alley is on fire, giving Caittina a chance to run un-
less the thugs are brave enough to cross the fire.
9.4. Size
Most games are centered on medium-sized characters,
which range from dwarves to elves. However creatures
and races both large and small are common in most
fantasy settings. Table 9-4a is used to determine the
size based on average weight and height or length of
the race. The primary consideration of size is weight,
however all members of a race are treated as the same
size. A human character does not get to be large just
because he weighs 300 lbs.
A creature or character’s total Hits are determined by
```
their Base Hits (Body Development + Racial) multi-
```
plied by the Hit Multiplier as shown in Table 9-4a.
This modification is applied when the character or
creature is created, so that it does not need to be calcu-
lated during combat.
Table 9-4a: Sizes
Size
Hit
Multiplier
Hits
```
Delivered* Weight (lbs)
```
Height /
Length Examples
I Miniscule x 0.25 x 1/4 < 1 mouse
II Diminutive x 0.50 x 1/2 1 - 4 < 6" pixie
III Tiny x 0.67 x 1/2 4 - 16 6" - 1' cat, hawk
IV Small x 0.75 x 1 16 - 64 1' - 4' dog, halfling
V Medium x 1 x 1 64 - 256 4' - 8' human, dwarf
VI Big x 1.5 x 1 256 - 1,024 8' - 15' pony, troll
VII Large x 2 x 2 1,024 - 4k 15' - 25' horse, wyvern
VIII Huge x 3 x 3 4k - 16k 25' - 50' elephant, rhino
IX Gigantic x 4 x 4 16k - 48k 50' - 100' triceratops
X Enormous x 5 x 5 48k+ 100' + apatosaur
```
+1 + (x 1) + (x 1) every x4
```
- if using the optional rule165


Arms Law
When a critical is inflicted, adjust the severity of the
critical based on the size of the attack and the size of
the defender, using the modifiers shown in Table 9-4b.
The net result is that larger combatants will inflict
more severe criticals against smaller foes, and smaller
foes will inflict less severe criticals against larger foes.
If a critical is reduced below an A severity, find the
next lower result that affects the same area of the body
```
on the A column and apply it (for more details, see
```
```
Section 9.8).
```
However, smaller combatants are also harder to hit.
For each size category smaller than the attack, they
```
gain a +5 DB modifier as noted in Table 9-5 (in the
```
```
next section). The size of a combatant's attack may be
```
modified from his normal size via charging, magical
or other effects. This affects the critical adjustment,
but not the DB modifier.
```
Example: A human (medium) attacks a halfling
```
```
(small) with a broadsword. Due to the size differ-
```
ence, the halfling gets a +5 DB bonus. The attack is
rolled, resulting in an 11C result. Since halflings are
small, the critical is increased one level from a C to
a D. The final attack result is a 11D. The Halfling
makes his broadsword attack and also gets an 11C
result, but the critical is reduced one level from a C
to a B. The final attack result is 11B.
```
Example: A human (medium) charging for a +1 size
```
```
increase (Section 8.2) with a two-handed claymore
```
```
(which attacks as a broadsword +1 size) attacks a
```
```
Troll (big). The attack size is therefore Large so the
```
critical severity is increased by two, but it is de-
creased by one due to the size of the troll. The target
is larger than the attacker, so there is no DB modifi-
er. If an 11C result is scored, it is increased to 11D.
Hits Adjustment Due to Size
Large creatures should deliver more Hits of damage than a
```
smaller creature if both are using the same attack (e.g., a claw
```
```
of a raven vs the claw of a wyvern). The multiplier in the “Hits
```
Delivered” column of Table 9-4a may be used to multiply the
hits delivered based on the size of the attack.
The Hits Delivered column differs from the Hit Multiplier col-
umn only for ease of on-the-fly calculations. If additional de-
tail is desired and multiplying by 2/3 or ¾ is acceptable, the Hit
Multiplier column may be used for hits delivered instead. The
```
included Small and Big tables incorporate these modifiers (x0.75
```
```
for Small, x1.5 for Big).
```
Range Adjustment Due to Size
Much as with hit adjustments, multiplying range increments by
the Hits Delivered or Hit Multiplier columns on the range table
will more accurately reflect the physics of larger and smaller
ranged weapons.
wRong-sized weapons
Combatants will normally prefer to wield weapons
that are properly suited to their size but in the heat of
battle may be forced to grab whatever is at hand. In
general, treat the attack as being of the weapon’s size
```
(e.g., a broadsword made for a halfling makes a Small
```
attack while a two-handed claymore made for a hal-
```
fling makes a Medium attack). A -20 penalty applies
```
when using a wrong-sized weapon.
A combatant may wield a smaller weapon in one hand,
even if it was designed for two-handed use. Even a
two-handed weapon made for a goblin will be smaller
and lighter than a typical one-handed weapon for a
human.
A combatant may wield a weapon that is one size larg-
er, if it was designed for one-handed use, but it will
require two hands. Larger two-handed weapons, or
any weapon more than one size too large, may not be
```
used at all. (As an exception, a dagger that is one size
```
too large is approximately the size of a broadsword, so
the GM may choose instead to treat it as a broadsword
```
of the character's size.)
```
```
Example: Bahs, a human wielding a two-handed
```
spear, is attacked by a troll wielding a one-handed
club. In an unlikely series of events, both combat-
ants fumble their weapons. The troll grabs the spear
while Bahs gets the club. Bahs picks up the 12 lbs
club with some difficulty, but manages to swing it
with both hands. It makes a Big attack at -20. The
troll finds the spear a bit puny and holds it in one
hand to also make a Big attack at -20.
Armor and shields made for larger or smaller creatures
are not normally usable without significant modifica-
tion by a craftsman.
Table 9-4b: Size Adjustments to Attacks
Attack Size
Critical
Adjustment
Defender
Size
Critical
Adjustment
Miniscule -4 Miniscule +4
Diminutive -3 Diminutive +3
Tiny -2 Tiny +2
Small -1 Small +1
Medium 0 Medium 0
Big +1 Big -1
Large +2 Large -2
Huge +3 Huge -3
Gigantic +4 Gigantic -4
Enormous +5 Enormous -5
```
(larger) (+1/size) (larger) (-1/size)
```
166166
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
9.5. Offensive Bonus
```
A character’s base Offensive Bonus (OB) is his skill
```
bonus with his weapon or attack, subject to various
modifiers. The modifiers vary depending on whether
it is a melee attack or a ranged attack as given in Table
9-5. In addition to these modifiers, all of the normal
modifiers to one’s skill apply as well:
```
•Penalties due to injury (e.g., losing 25% or more of
```
one’s hits or suffering a penalty to all actions due
```
to a critical) reduce the attacker’s OB.
```
•If the attacker uses less than the maximum Action
Points for his attack, he suffers the normal penalty
of -25 for each Action Point less than the maxi-
mum used.
•If the attacker is moving while attacking, he suffers
```
a Pace Penalty (Section 8.2: Tactical Movement).
```
•Penalties due to fatigue, from failed Endurance rolls.
facing and flanking
Each combatant has four facings: front, two flanks
```
(left and right), and rear. A character will normally
```
```
only attack (or defend against) an opponent in his
```
front facing, although with a penalty he can attack
other facings. For humanoids facing equal-sized op-
ponents, there are six positions around a combatant:
```
three in front (180 degrees), two flanks (left and right
```
```
60 degrees each), and a single rear facing (60 degrees).
```
If a character is moving, his front facing is determined
by the direction of travel. Otherwise, if a character is
making an attack or parrying, his facing is usually de-
```
termined by the position of the target (i.e., as the target
```
moves, the character may turn to face the target imme-
```
diately during the target’s movement). When facing
```
multiple opponents, a character can choose which
one to follow, although with intelligent foes working
together this will nearly always mean one foe will get
a flank or rear attack. When in doubt, resolve with a
conflicted Running absolute maneuver against each
```
additional foe; Failure means they are to your rear,
```
Success on your flank, and Absolute Success permits
you to keep them in your front arc.
Melee combatants attacking from the flank or rear re-
```
ceive a bonus (+15 for flank or +35 for rear), and in
```
addition, the foe cannot use a shield against the attack.
Sometimes, however, a combatant may wish to attack
or parry against an opponent to his flank or even rear
facing. For example, a combatant might find himself
stuck between a swordsman and an archer. He can
```
choose to face the archer (to gain the benefits of his
```
```
shield) while attacking the swordsman now in his rear
```
facing. Attacks made to the flank or rear suffer a penal-
```
ty of twice the positional modifier (to the flank is -30,
```
```
to the rear is -70).
```
The Combat Expertise: Reverse Strike skill can be
used to offset the penalties to attacks declared against
targets to the flank or rear, or it can reduce the posi-
tional bonus an enemy would receive when attacking
```
at a ratio of 2:1 (for every +2 bonus in Reverse Strike
```
```
the positional modifier can be reduced by 1). If used
```
against multiple foes, first divide the character’s skill
```
by the number of foes (rounding down), then reduce
```
the penalties. In order to use the skill in this way the
combatant must be actively in combat and be aware of
the attacker.
```
Example: Anika has a Reverse Strike skill of +70
```
and is being attacked by one thief from behind, but
cannot turn around because she stepped in a glue
trap on the floor. Anika could reduce the thief’s rear
```
facing bonus to 0 (half her Reverse Strike bonus of
```
```
+70 is equal to the rear attack bonus of +35), or
```
Anika could instead choose to let the thief get the
full rear bonus, and reduce her own attack penalty
```
to (70 – 70) = 0, or any combination thereof.
```
167
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
RestRicted QUaRteRs
As a general rule, if a combatant is fighting in an area
smaller than his melee range, he should suffer a penalty
due to restricted quarters. This is due to a smaller area
to maneuver in, difficulty in swinging a weapon, etc.
Clearance is not as important as one’s ability to maneu-
ver, but for some long weapons it can be a more serious
problem.
It is ultimately up to the Gamemaster to determine the
penalty based on how restricted the combat is, taking
into account the size of the room or area, allies, foes,
ceiling height, furniture, weapon type being wielded,
etc. There are four categories of Restricted Quarters:
```
Close (-25), Cramped (-50), Tight (-75), and Confined
```
```
(-100). The Restricted Quarters penalties could also ap-
```
ply to the use of ranged weapons, such as the use
of a sling in a low cave. The Battle Expertise:
Restricted Quarters Fighting skill can be used to
offset these penalties.
```
Example: Jakko, a human warrior, chases
```
some goblins into a cave. Jakko is 6' tall with a
```
4' sword, so his melee range is (6/2) + 4 = 7'.
```
At first the 10' cave is fine, but as he continues
to chase the goblins, it narrows to 6' high and
wide. Now his head is brushing the ceiling, he
cannot raise his sword over his head, and he
has to be wary of clipping walls. The GM de-
cides this is “close” and imposes a -25 penalty.
Chasing the goblins further into a 4' high and
wide section, Jakko is now crouched over, and
his swing is further impeded to “cramped” and
-50. Further on he enters into a 3' high and
wide section, and is now using one hand to half
crawl and reduced to waving his blade in nar-
row arcs for “tight” or -75. Finally he crawls
into a 2' high and wide section, he moves on his
belly, and he is reduced to cramped stabbing
motions, so the GM rules the conditions “con-
fined” or -100. Note that the goblins would
```
likely have no problems in the 6' section (unless
```
```
wielding long pole arms), but even they would
```
be struggling with restricted quarters by the
time the cave is reduced to 2'.
sURpRised oR flatfooted
When executing attacks on surprised foes, the
attacker gets +25 OB, in addition to any posi-
```
tional modifiers (if melee). Against flatfooted
```
foes, the bonus is +60: +25 for surprise and
+35 for an automatic rear attack, regardless
```
of position. (See Section 8.3 for the definition
```
```
of surprise and flatfooted.) This represents
```
the combatant being able to freely hit his foe
wherever desired, even if in front. In addition,
a flatfooted foe cannot benefit from a shield, although
any magical or armor quality bonuses would still ap-
ply. Note that ranged weapons do not get the automat-
ic rear attack bonus since ranged weapons do not get
positional modifiers. Surprised foes still get DB due to
Quickness, but flatfooted combatants do not.
```
Example: Ord, an assassin of vile disposition, has
```
become angered during a card game with the ev-
er-cheerful Mhairi. Determined to have his revenge,
Ord bides his time and ambushes Mhairi in the dead
of night as she walks home. Ord catches the slightly
drunk Mhairi completely flatfooted, giving him a
bonus of +60. Fortunately for the ever-lucky Mhai-
ri, poor Ord rolls and fumbles his sword, dropping
it on his foot and alerting Mhairi. Foiled again!

However, Passive Dodge and Passive Block are not cumulative.

168
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
9.6. Defensive Bonus
```
A combatant’s Defensive Bonus (DB) represents
```
dodging, parrying, shield use, and tactical advantag-
es. A combatant’s DB is determined by the factors in
Table 9-5. As long as a combatant is able to move it
always get its DB from Quickness, unless caught flat-
footed. A flying combatant gets a bonus to its DB even
in melee, as the extra dimension of movement is use-
ful in dodging attacks. To use a shield, the combatant
must be aware of the attack and be using the shield to
defend against it.
It is possible to have negative DB due to a Quickness
penalty. This does not represent foes jumping into the
path of an arrow. Rather, the attack tables assume an
average foe who is actively trying to not get hit. If
the combatant is deficient in some way, attacks will be
more effective against it than against an average foe.
coveR
Walls, curtains, fog and other combatants can all make
it difficult to target a foe. Cover can provide DB modifi-
ers against both melee and ranged attacks, and these are
given in Table 9-5. Note that cover provides twice the
modification against ranged attacks as it does against
melee.
Hard cover consists of anything that can stop the attack
```
outright (e.g., walls and trees) or deflect the shot (e.g.,
```
```
fences and bushes). Soft cover consists of anything that
```
obscures vision, but does not interfere with the attack
```
(e.g., fog, darkness, curtains). The GM is the final arbi-
```
ter on how much cover an obstacle offers and whether
the cover is hard or soft cover, based on the nature of
the attack and the obstacle being used for cover.
To determine the amount of cover, consider how much
of the defender is blocked from the attacker’s view by
the cover. If the attacker cannot see a quarter of the
target, that is partial cover, if he cannot see half of the
target, that is half cover, and if the target is 90% or more
obscured, that qualifies as full cover. Small characters
should be able to more easily find sufficient cover to
protect themselves, while Big characters may have
trouble finding anything big enough to hide behind.
```
Another combatant (enemy or ally) counts as hard cov-
```
er if someone attempts to attack past one combatant to
strike another combatant. The amount of cover is based
on positioning and the relative size of the combatants.
For example, attacks from the second rank are normally
considered half hard cover, for a +40 DB bonus. Pole
arms may be used more effectively when striking past
```
cover and reduce the cover bonuses by half (i.e., half
```
```
hard cover results in only +20 DB).
```
dodging
A combatant may attempt to dodge out of the
way of attacks, increasing their Defensive Bonus.
There are three types of dodging, representing differ-
ent amounts of effort spent on defense. A combatant
may benefit from only one type of dodging in a round.
All types of dodging add to DB against all melee
foes the combatant is aware of for the entire round.
Dodging can also be used against ranged attacks, but
the DB value is halved, as the combatant must move
away from where they anticipate the attack will go, or
simply move in an unpredictable manner in order to be
```
difficult to target; these attacks are typically too fast to
```
focus on dodging the projectile itself. Dodging is not
possible at all against area attacks.
Dodging utilizes the ranks and bonus of one of the
Movement skills, depending on the environment. In
normal land-based encounters use Running, in the wa-
ter use Swimming, and while climbing use Climbing.
If the combatant is currently moving using Acrobatics,
the GM may allow that to be used as the appropriate
Movement skill.
Passive dodging takes no action and adds the combat-
ant’s ranks in the appropriate Movement skill to their
DB, up to a maximum of +50 DB. Passive dodging
may not be combined with DB granted from passive
use of a shield. Using the shield involves its own type
of footwork as is represented by the Shield skill.
Partial dodging requires concentration and adds half
the combatant’s appropriate Movement skill to their
DB.
Full dodging takes 4 Action Points and adds the com-
batant’s full appropriate Movement skill to their DB.
Encumbrance and armor penalties greatly hinder
dodging as they apply to the combatant's gained DB.
These penalties apply in full to passive, partial, and
full dodging, which means any moderate encumbrance
or armor is often enough to nullify the much smaller
passive bonus. If dodging ranged attacks, subtract all
penalties first before halving the DB.
The DB bonuses for partial and full dodging are re-
duced by any injury penalties which the character is
suffering. As with other actions, partial and full dodg-
ing are also reduced by the pace penalty if the charac-
ter is moving.
169
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
paRRying
Combat in Rolemaster is quite deadly, and using parry
is one of the best ways for a combatant to stay alive.
Combatants who try to go full offense and never parry
will be quite dangerous for the short time before they
take a lethal critical and die. Generally, only mind-
less or berserk combatants will completely disregard
danger in order to strike out at a foe with all of their
offense.
A combatant may trade offensive capability for de-
fense by spending some of his available Offensive Bo-
nus to parry a foe when attacked. His DB is increased
by an equal amount and counts against all melee at-
tacks from that foe for the round. If he has no OB left,
because it was already spent on an attack or parrying
others, then he may not parry. No penalties apply to
the parry DB directly, but injury penalties, pace, and
other penalties will reduce the available Offensive Bo-
nus and therefore limit the amount of parrying that is
possible.
If the combatant who is parrying is making multiple
attacks that round, his OB for all his attacks is reduced
by the same amount, although it is only added to the
DB once.
As long as the last Action Point a combatant spent
on actions was on a melee action, he may parry any
```
foe who attacks him in melee. (Spending an Action
```
Point on movement does not interrupt melee or pre-
```
vent parrying.) If the attacker is to his rear or flank the
```
```
OB is penalized by the positional modifier (Table 9-5)
```
before any amount of OB is diverted to DB, although
that penalty may be offset by the Combat Expertise:
Reverse Strike skill.
```
Example: Niles is training several young monks
```
and one of them is behind him. Niles’ unarmed OB
is +90, of which he will parry with 50. Normally,
this would be completely eliminated due to the -70
```
penalty for attacks (and parry) to the rear, but Niles
```
has +50 Reverse Strike skill. The penalty is reduced
to -20, so 30 of his parry will be applied against the
young monk’s attack.
A combatant may attempt to parry more than one foe,
reducing the OB by the desired amount against each
foe. An Absolute maneuver roll is required for each
```
foe to be parried after the first (using the combatant’s
```
```
Combat Training skill), and with a Success or higher
```
```
the foe may be parried (otherwise no effect). A maneu-
```
ver roll is allowed for each additional foe even if an
earlier such roll was failed. If a combatant uses all of
his OB for parrying, so that his OB is zero by the time
his attack would resolve, no attack is made.
```
Example: Anika is fighting a wolf with two attacks
```
```
(bite and claw). Anika’s OB is +100 with her
```
falchion. She chooses to parry the wolf with 30.
As a result, her OB is reduced to +70, but her DB
increases by 30 against both attacks. The goblin rid-
ing the wolf is also attacking with a spear. If Anika
succeeds in an Absolute maneuver with her Blades
skill, she could decide to parry the goblin with +20,
but that would reduce her OB to +50. Decisions,
decisions.
Restricted Parrying
In some situations, it can be very difficult to parry,
such as an unarmed combatant parrying an opponent
with a weapon. If parrying is restricted, only 1 point of
```
DB is added for every 2 points of OB spent (round up).
```
All other parry rules apply as normal. If multiple sit-
uations apply to make parrying exceptionally difficult
the GM may rule that parrying cannot be applied at all.
Restricted parrying includes the following situations:
•An unarmed combatant parrying against someone
with a weapon, unless the unarmed combatant has
```
an item or armor with which to parry (e.g., brac-
```
```
ers, small shield, or a large metal mug).
```
•A combatant fighting an invisible foe, because he
cannot see blows coming, so he can only guess at
where to parry to cover vital areas.
•A combatant fighting an incorporeal foe because
blocking is ineffective and he can only rely on
motion to avoid getting hit.
•GM discretion: The Gamemaster may rule additional
```
situations that restrict one’s ability to parry (e.g.,
```
a small halfling with a dagger parrying a gigantic
```
dragon claw attack).
```
```
Example: Xavier, having just broken his heirloom
```
sword, is left with nothing but his own hands and
his skill at Martial Arts Strikes when he is set upon
by a fearsome war mattock-wielding ogre! Because
he has nothing with which to parry, Xavier is re-
stricted in his parrying. He decides to parry 80,
which gives him +40 DB instead of the normal +80.
If he had been more thoughtful, he might have held
```
onto the hilt of his broken sword; it would have been
```
enough to use for more effective parrying.
170
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
shields
Like armor, the use of shields is varied, from small
target shields to large wall shields weighing 20 lbs.
Larger shields can protect the most but are bulky and
heavy. Very skilled combatants can use even smaller
shields to great effect, especially when confronted
with a single foe.
A shield may be used against one attack which the
combatant is aware of and facing in his front arc.
Larger shields may be used against more than one at-
tack, up to a maximum number of attacks based on the
shield type as shown in Table 9-6. For each attack be-
yond the first, a maneuver roll must be made using the
combatant’s Shield skill. On a Success or higher the
DB may be applied, otherwise it is not. A failed roll
still counts against the maximum number of attacks.
Shield DB may be applied against melee, missile, and
directed spell attacks.
Additionally, a combatant may add his Shield skill
ranks to his DB, for a maximum of +50 DB due to
```
shield (including the base shield DB). This bonus ap-
```
plies against the same attacks as the base shield DB.
This passive blocking may not be combined with pas-
sive dodging, as using the shield involves its own type
of footwork as is represented by the Shield skill.
By spending actions, a shield may be used even more
effectively to block melee or ranged attacks. Partial
blocking requires concentration and adds half the
combatant’s Shield skill to their DB. Full blocking
takes 4 Action Points and adds the combatant’s full
Shield skill to their DB. The bonuses for partial and
full blocking are reduced by any injury penalties the
character has sustained, and by any pace penalty if the
character is moving. As above, a Shield maneuver roll
is required for each attack after the first. Only one type
```
of blocking (partial, full, or passive) may be used per
```
round.
```
A shield used to attack (a shield bash) does not pro-
```
vide any DB whatsoever for the round.
```
Example: Anika has a full shield, 15 ranks of Shield
```
skill, and a bonus of +78. She uses her shield
against the spear of a goblin and the bite of the wolf
he is riding upon, as well as an arrow fired by a
goblin archer. She intends to attack, so she chooses
to only benefit from passive blocking. Anika’s DB
increases by 25 + 15 = 40. She gains this benefit
for the first attack automatically, but must succeed
in a Shield absolute maneuver for each of the other
attacks to use the shield against them.
```
Example: Later, Anika is facing three goblin ar-
```
chers. She chooses a full block as she is advancing
on their position, costing her 4 AP but hopefully
keeping her alive long enough to attack them next
round. Her bonus is 25 + 78 = +103, but, as before,
she must succeed in a Shield absolute maneuver to
defend against the second and third arrows. Never-
theless, she is grateful she left her little target shield
at home and brought the full shield!
Weapons and Items as “Shields”
Weapons, or other suitably robust items, can be used
for passive defense. A combatant can use a sword to
attack and parry, and a stool in the off-hand as addi-
tional defense. If not used to attack or parry, items
give a +5 DB vs. one melee attack. Such items cannot
be used with Shield skill for passive, partial, or full
blocking. Waving a dagger around will not protect you
from incoming arrows! It is best to find cover, move
evasively, or find a shield.
Table 9-6: Shields
Type DB Max Attacks
Target +15 1
Normal +20 2
Full +25 3
Wall +30 4
171
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
9.7. Special Maneuvers
called shots
Sometimes a combatant may wish to attack a spe-
cific location on the body, such as the head. The
```
five locations on the critical tables are head (in-
```
```
cluding neck), arms, chest, abdomen (including
```
```
groin), and legs, and limbs each have a right and
```
a left. Making a called shot incurs a penalty due to the
difficulty of hitting the specific location.
Against same-sized humanoids, a called shot usually
incurs a penalty of -25 to the attack roll. The GM may
decide in some circumstances to impose a greater or
```
lesser penalty (from -50 to -10). For example, a human
```
melee attacking a giant’s right leg with a dagger may
only be a -10, while attacking his head may be -50.
Called shots are most useful when the targeted area is
less armored than the remainder of the body. If the tar-
geted area is armored differently from the torso, treat
the attack as being against AT 1 for no armor, AT 3 for
light armor, AT 5 for medium armor, and AT 9 for heavy
armor.
If the attack is successful, then the attack has hit the
specified location. If a critical is inflicted, make the
critical roll as normal, but if the rolled result indicates
a different location, keep checking lesser results on that
column until you reach one that affects the called body
area, and resolve using that result. If there is no such
lesser row, then the strike was on target but mostly inef-
```
fective; use the result on the column for an 01 result and
```
modify the text to fit. The Groin & Abdomen result on a
```
66 cannot be obtained by this shift (it may still be used
```
```
if rolled naturally); use the 26-35 result instead.
```
disaRming
A disarming attack seeks to force an opponent to drop
his weapon, or even take it from him. Disarming at-
tempts are resolved as a conflicting maneuver, with the
```
disarmer rolling as if it were a normal attack (including
```
```
any penalty from the target parrying the attacker), with
```
additional penalties as indicated on Table 9-7a. The
```
defending foe makes a defense roll (which does not re-
```
```
quire an action), as if it were an attack roll (subtracting
```
```
any parry used), with any amount over 100 used as an
```
additional penalty to the attacker’s disarm roll or any
amount under 100 applied as a bonus. The result is in-
dicated on Table 9-7a.
Learning techniques to disarm can help in the disarm-
ing attempt. Combatants can use the Combat Expertise:
Disarming skill bonus to reduce the disarming penalties
```
(except for the foe’s defense roll), to a minimum pen-
```
alty of 0.
The penalties for disarming a 2-H weapon, the foe’s
defense roll, and using a ranged weapon are cumulative
with all other penalties.
```
Disarming cannot be used against natural attacks; at
```
best one can make a called shot on that body part and
hope to incapacitate or sever the attacking limb.
```
Example: The angry peasant rushes at Meretor with
```
a wooden club and misinformed accusations. Em-
barrassed but with no desire to harm the peasant,
Meretor attempts to disarm him instead. Meretor is
```
armed with a broadsword (-75 for disarming). His
```
```
melee skill is +87 and his Disarm skill is +40 (re-
```
```
ducing the penalty to -35). He puts faith in his abil-
```
ities and elects not to parry, so his total is +52. He
rolls 41. The peasant has +20 melee skill and rolls a
30. Since the peasant’s roll has fallen under 100, the
```
amount below 100 (50) is added to Meretor’s roll
```
for a total of 143. The peasant’s club tumbles to the
ground.
Table 9-7a: Disarm Maneuvers
Roll Result
< 1 Absolute Failure
Your attempt to disarm your foe is laughable. Not only do you
fail to come close to disarming him, but you immediately fum-
ble your weapon.
1 - 75 Failure
Sigh. Some days it just seems like the world is out to get you.
You miss your opportunity. You fail to disarm. You fail to at-
tack. You just fail. You're a failure.
UM 66 Unusual Event
Weird… it seems your foe is trying to disarm you, perhaps in-
advertently, at the same time. If you fail this maneuver, your foe
has disarmed you. If you succeed, you still manage to disarm
your foe, but drop your weapon as well.
76 - 100 Partial Success
You can't partially disarm someone! Instead treat the disarm as
a normal attack using this roll, but using all normal modifiers
```
(and parry).
```
101 - 175 Success
You successfully disarm your foe! The weapon falls to the
```
ground (and must make a breakage check), but is within melee
```
range.
176 + Absolute Success
You make disarming look easy as your foe gawks at you as
you expertly remove him from his weapon. You may choose
between taking the weapon for yourself or throw it up to 10' in
a direction of your choice.
Modifiers
-50 Unarmed/grapple/crush attack
-75 Blade attack
-100 Piercing attack
-100 Ranged attack*
-25 Disarming a 2-H weapon*
- cumulative with other penalties
172
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
fiRing into melee
There is nothing preventing a combatant from firing
or throwing a weapon into melee, whether to hit a foe
amongst allies or pick out a specific foe in a battle.
Based on the position of the attacker, defender, and
other combatants the defender may gain cover from
the other combatants.
```
Example: Two characters are in melee. An archer
```
is trying to shoot past his ally to hit an enemy. The
GM decides that the target has partial hard cover
based on the positions of the three combatants, and
this gives the target +40 DB for cover.
If a ranged attack misses its intended target then the
attack may randomly hit one of the combatants that
provided cover. In this case, make a second attack roll
at +0 skill OB against the secondary target. The new
```
target (select randomly if more than one) does not gain
```
the benefit of any cover modifiers from other combat-
ants. Fumbles are ignored on the second attack roll.
katas
Most often, a combatant is wielding a single weap-
on and attacking a single opponent. However, on
occasion, a combatant may be using multiple attacks
```
together (multiple weapons or natural attacks). These
```
simultaneous attacks are often learned by practicing
combinations of moves that allow the motions of the
attacks to flow together, each alongside or into the
next, referred to as katas. These moves are performed
either simultaneously or in rapid succession, allowing
multiple attacks to be resolved.
To execute multiple attacks, the use of each attack
must be justified with respect to a given limb, with no
limb used as the basis for more than one attack. For
example, a combatant wielding two weapons, with
one in each hand, may strike once with each of them.
A combatant could also make a martial arts sweep
```
with a leg or a strike with the head (i.e., a head butt or
```
```
horns), but cannot use either to make a dagger attack
```
unless they have somehow mounted blades on their
boot and helmet, which would be awkward enough to
add a significant penalty to OB.
A weapon wielded in a combatant’s off-hand suffers a
```
-20 penalty (unless they have the Ambidextrous talent)
```
and uses the same skill as that weapon in their primary
hand. Natural, unarmed attacks, and shield attacks do
not suffer any penalty for use of the off-hand.
When making two simultaneous attacks, there is a
penalty of -75 to each attack. For each attack beyond
two, this penalty increases by -25, as shown in Table
9-7b. Furthermore, there is a penalty of -20 for each
opponent beyond the first, also applied to all attacks.
```
The penalties (including the multiple opponent penal-
```
```
ty) can be offset by the combatant’s Combat Expertise:
```
Multiple Attacks skill.
The penalties for katas are also offset by modifiers
for light weapons, but note that the total modifier can
never result in a net bonus to the attack, only in can-
celling out the penalties. A light weapon is a dagger,
```
unarmed attack, natural weapon (e.g., claws, bite),
```
```
or any weapon with a -1 size adjustment (e.g., epee,
```
```
machete, hand axe). If the combatant is making more
```
than two attacks, the bonus for a light off-hand weap-
on only applies once, and only if all attacks beyond the
first are qualified. For example, a combatant attacking
with a sword, dagger, and a kick would receive the
+20 bonus, but a combatant striking with two swords
and a kick would not.
Note that while katas allow multiple attacks as part
of a single attack action, a combatant may also make
multiple attacks simply by performing multiple attack
actions, either by using fewer Action Points for each
```
attack or if they have extra Action Points (e.g., due to
```
```
a Haste effect). The penalties on table 9-7b apply only
```
to attacks made as part of the same attack action.
```
Example: Kulahren wants to attack one foe with his
```
dagger and another with his rapier. Two attacks are
-75 each, and attacking two foes gives an addition-
al -20. This -95 is reduced by Kulahren’s Multiple
```
Attack skill of +59 (including a +20 bonus because
```
```
his off-hand weapon is a dagger) resulting in a -95
```
- 59 = -36 to both attacks. If he wanted to attack
only one foe with both weapons they would each
have a penalty of -75 + 59 = -16 instead.
Table 9-7b: Katas
# Attacks Modifier
2 -75
3 -100
4 -125
5 -150
Per additional foe -20 each
Primary weapon is light +10
Off-hand weapon is:
Dagger or any natural weapon +20
Any other light weapon +10
173
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
moUnted combat
Being mounted provides an enormous tactical advantage.
Mounted combatants benefit from the animal’s speed and
mass when charging, as well as a higher ground bonus
when they are fighting similarly-sized opponents on
foot. Charging attacks made by mounted opponents are
treated as the size of the mount plus any increases due to
```
charging speed (based on the BMR of the mount). The
```
mount and rider are considered separate combatants,
with separate initiatives and actions. Fumbles made in
mounted combat should be rolled on the ‘Mounted Com-
bat’ column.
However, riders attacking from a moving mount are sub-
ject to a Pace Penalty based on the pace of the mount
```
(-100 for Sprint, -125 for Dash). These penalties can
```
be reduced with the Battle Expertise: Mounted Combat
skill.
Controlling a mount is assumed to be a negligible activ-
```
ity under normal circumstances; however, the GM may
```
require a Riding maneuver for unusual maneuvers or
situations. Riders with zero riding skill ranks are the ex-
ception to this rule, and attempts to make attacks from a
moving mount should require a medium riding maneuver
at the normal -25 no skill penalty plus mount pace each
round an attack is declared from a moving mount, with
failure resulting in the rider falling off.
Note that mounts not trained for combat will very likely
panic and flee when fighting breaks out. Rules for mount
morale and fear are covered in Section 14.3.
pRotecting otheRs
Sometimes a combatant may want to parry or use a
shield to protect an ally. He can do so by sacrificing
some offensive capability to impose himself between the
other combatants. The protector must be within melee
range of both of the combatants, and gets a penalty to
his OB based on his position as indicated on Table 9-7c.
The penalties can be offset by the combatant’s Battle
```
Expertise: Protect skill. The protector may designate any
```
part of his parry or shield DB as protecting the defended
combatant instead of himself. The protector may wait
until the attack is declared before deciding to do so.
```
Example: Anika and Talen are side by side facing a
```
massive ogre with a +150 OB. They both allocate
80 to parry, which is probably not enough to stop
the ogre’s blow. However, as the ogre begins to
swing his huge axe at Talen, Anika decides she will
protect her ally. She has +25 in the Protect skill,
offsetting half the penalty of -50. Thus, Anika takes
```
a -25 to her OB (lowering her parry to +55) and
```
parries the remainder on behalf of Talen. Talen now
```
has a DB of 135 (80 from his own parry + 55 from
```
```
Anika). Talen now has a decent chance to survive,
```
but Anika has left herself open to any attacks direct-
ed toward her.
Table 9-7c: Protect
Scenario Modifier
Protector between Attacker and Defender -25
Defender between Attacker and Protector -100
All other scenarios -50
174
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
slaying attacks
```
Some attacks (often magi-
```
```
cal) are exceptionally dan-
```
gerous and carry a higher
chance of severe injury.
Similarly, some creatures
may be particularly vul-
nerable to a type of attack
```
(e.g., fire attacks vs. an
```
```
ice elemental). These are
```
referred to as Slaying at-
tacks and are one of the
few effects that give a direct bonus to the critical roll
as indicated in Table 9-7d. A Slaying weapon or effect
is given a Slaying Type from I – V. Creatures who are
vulnerable will state that specific attacks are treated as
Slaying and also give the type.
```
Example: A broadsword +5 of orc Slaying II will get
```
+10 to any critical rolls against orcs.
```
Example: A fire elemental takes Slaying II from wa-
```
ter attacks, thus all water based attacks get +10 to
the critical roll.
sUbdUal
Combatants may attempt non-lethal attacks that are
intended to knock out or otherwise subdue a foe with-
out killing him. Subdual attacks inflict more non-le-
thal injuries such as stuns and fatigue, although there
is still a risk of seriously injuring or killing the foe.
Some weapons are more suitable for use in subduing
than others. A subdual attack is treated like a normal
attack, except that the modifiers in Table 9-7e apply
based on the type of weapon or attack used. Some
weapons may be specifically designed with hooks,
pincers or wrapping elements to make them more suit-
ed to use in subdual than is described here. Most such
```
specialty weapons inflict a grapple critical (which
```
```
subdues in its own way), but modifications to standard
```
```
weapons (such as adding a blunt hook to the back of
```
```
a pole axe) might make them suited to lesser penalties
```
than are given in the table at the discretion of the GM.
Learning to use more precise blows, combined with
knowledge of target anatomy can help render an op-
ponent immobile or unconscious. The penalties can be
offset by the combatant's Combat Expertise: Subdual
skill.
Most humanoids are assumed to share a common
enough anatomy that the unfamiliar anatomy penalty
would not apply. For other known races and creatures,
the Subdual skill is assumed to include subdual tech-
niques, or at least a rough knowledge of vulnerable
points, so the penalty applies but may be canceled
out using Subdual skill. However, for completely
unknown races or creatures, the unfamiliar anatomy
penalty cannot be offset by the Subdual skill.
Subdual attacks do half concussion hit damage and
use the Subdual critical table instead of the normal
critical type, with a maximum severity of E.
```
Example: The ever-talented Karn has a Subdual skill
```
bonus of +100. In melee combat, he could make
a subdual attack with any type of weapon without
penalty. However, Karn’s favorite mode of attack is
from afar. He will often try to subdue using a short
bow, with which he gets a -50 to all subdual attacks.
```
(-100 for piercing, -50 for ranged, total penalty of
```
```
-150 +100 bonus for a final penalty of -50.)
```
toUch attacks
While there are very few offensive spells with a range
of “touch,” there are a couple of fairly common situ-
ations in which a caster will wish to touch a target to
deliver a spell in a combat situation. The first case is
when the caster wishes to touch a target in order to
```
gain the -15 RR modifier for a range of “Touch“ (see
```
```
Spell Law, Table 4-5). The second case is when the
```
caster wishes to make contact with an ally in order
to deliver a touch-ranged Utility spell. Any unarmed
combat skill can be used to attempt to deliver a spell
touch to a target engaged in melee, with any result
of 1 hit or more indicating successful delivery of the
spell by contact. The caster can choose to only attempt
to “tag“ the target to deliver the spell, in which case
no actual damage is done, but that must be declared
before the attack is rolled. While a friendly target will
```
likely not actively defend (parry, apply a shield, etc.)
```
against the caster's attempted touch, it is still difficult
to make solid contact with an active combatant, who
is moving unpredictably in an environment where the
caster must also look to his own safety, so DB due to
Quickness, magical armor, applicable spells, etc. still
applies. A foe who has declared parry on the caster
gets full benefit of the parry to their DB for this type
of attack.
Table 9-7e: Subdual
Attack Type Modifier
Unarmed, blackjack, sap -25
```
Crushing (mace, club, ice bolt) -50
```
```
Blades (with flat side), claws -75
```
```
Piercing (bite, stinger) -100
```
Ranged* -50
Unfamiliar Anatomy* -50
- cumulative with other penalties
Table 9-7d: Slaying
Type Crit Bonus
I +5
II +10
III +15
IV +20
V +25

ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
9.8. Criticals & Injuries
An injury is all of the hits inflicted as well as criticals
delivered as the result of an attack, failed maneuver,
```
or spell. Hits represent light injuries (bruises, cuts),
```
general pain, minor blood loss, and the will to keep
fighting. Criticals represent types of damage causing
more serious wounds that can include stun, broken
and severed limbs, and major blood loss.
There are a variety of critical tables representing dif-
```
ferent types of damage (e.g., Slash, Puncture, Heat,
```
```
etc.), each with 5 levels of severity from A to E. A
```
criticals are the least severe and usually result in minor
damage, with an incapacitating injury or death only
```
occurring in very rare cases (i.e., a roll of 100). E criti-
```
cals tend to be moderate to severe, with a good chance
of death or incapacitation, although minor blows can
result. Each critical result will give a description and a
```
series of numbers and symbols. (e.g., “Foe reels from
```
```
the blow. +5 hits; stunned 1 round”).
```
```
Criticals are resolved with a straight d100 (non-open-
```
```
ended) roll, usually without modifiers. Slaying at-
```
tacks, Ambush attacks, exceptional attacks over 175,
and possibly magical effects can modify the critical
roll, but these tend to be rare.
Although the critical tables have a maximum severity
of E, the attack tables may indicate results as severe
as J. Criticals higher than an E do an E critical plus a
secondary critical as shown in Table 9-8a. Depending
on the type of attack the type of the secondary critical
```
(e.g., Slash, Krush, etc.) may not be the same. If the at-
```
tack table lists multiple types of criticals, they are used
in the order listed. If multiple criticals are delivered
against a target for any reason, all criticals always use
the same roll and modifiers, and consequently will hit
```
the same target location (so a flaming sword will cut
```
and burn the target’s arm, rather than cutting his arm
```
and burning his foot).
```
The severity of a critical can also be increased or de-
creased due to size differences or other factors. If a
critical is reduced below an A severity, find the next
lower result that affects the same area of the body on
the A column and apply it. If the critical is reduced
more than 1 below an A, move down again to the next
lower result for each step less than A. If there is no
```
lower result for that area then use the lowest (01 roll)
```
critical result and change the description as necessary.
```
Example: An A Krush critical is scored and the roll
```
is 70, but the severity is reduced due to use of a
small weapon against a medium target. A result of
```
67-75 is a Leg hit, so the next lower Leg result (for
```
```
36-45) is used instead: “Weak delivery, but great
```
timing. Foe caught off balance.”
```
Example: Another A Krush critical is rolled in the
```
same circumstance, with a result of 8, another Leg
shot. There is no lesser Leg result, so the result of
1 is used instead. Since it indicates “Blow barely
grazes foe’s ear,” the GM tells the player “the blow
barely grazes your knee” instead.
When an area effect causes criticals to multiple tar-
gets, the result for each target should be rolled sepa-
rately, so it is possible
for a Fire Ball to burn
one target’s arm and
another target’s foot.
The GM may choose
to roll a single time
for a large group, or
for sections of a large
group, to save time.
```
(I.e., if boiling water pours over a hundred soldiers
```
inflicting a B Heat critical on each of them, the GM
may choose to roll one critical for all 100, or perhaps
```
five groups of 20 to keep some variation.)
```
Table 9-8a: High Criticals
F E + A
G E + B
H E + C
I E + C + A
J E + C + B
176
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
inteRpReting cRiticals
Critical results assume a humanoid opponent, which
can easily be adjusted for most beings. Across all crit-
ical tables and severities, for a given roll, the location
```
is always the same (e.g., a roll of 56-65 is always an
```
```
arm hit). In the case more guidance is needed, use the
```
rough rules below.
•Location: Criticals occur in one of 5 locations: arm,
```
leg, head, chest, and abdomen (lower abdomen
```
```
and groin). It is often necessary to know on which
```
```
side of the body the hit has been scored (partic-
```
```
ularly with arm and leg hits). If the unmodified
```
attack roll is odd, the hit has been on the foe’s left
side, while if the unmodified attack roll is even,
the hit has been on his right side.
•Death: Any critical that specifies death should be
considered as causing death, regardless of the tar-
get. For example, if a skeleton who does not have
a brain receives a critical result that says “brain is
destroyed,” the skeleton’s head is destroyed and it
is “dead” anyway. Any specific exceptions to this
rule will be noted in the creature’s description in
Creature Law.
•Multiples of the same limb pair: If the foe has mul-
tiple sets of arms or legs, determine the side using
the left-right rule, then randomly determine which
limb was hit.
•Non-humanoids: If the foe is non-humanoid then
shift the location to the most analogous location
on the foe. A quadruped’s hind legs would be leg
locations while the front legs would be arm loca-
tions. An octopus’ tentacles would be the leg and
```
arm locations (determine left-right then random-
```
```
ize).
```
•No such location: Snakes, amoebas, and certain
other creatures might not have a specific location
that matches the given critical location. In these
cases, change the text of the critical to affect the
torso or other appropriate part, while keeping the
actual results of the critical.
•Tail: Tails are sensitive areas, being an extension
of the spine, so the tail is considered part of the
abdomen. If it is plausible that the tail has been hit
```
in combat (e.g., frontal attack in a narrow corridor
```
```
is not), use odd/even to determine abdomen/tail.
```
For a 66 result, randomly determine if it is the ab-
domen or tail.
•Wings: For birds and other creatures with no arms,
wings will equate to arms. However, if the foe has
arms and wings, use the left-right rule then ran-
domly determine if it is an arm or a wing.
Criticals can cause a variety of effects from fatigue
to bleeding to long-term damage or death. The text
of a critical will describe the hit, and sometimes a
specific effect such as dropping an item. All types of
standard critical results are described below, although
some critical results may additionally describe a more
unique effect.
bleeding
Bleeding too serious to stop on its own will cause
additional hits every round until death or until it is
stopped through first aid or magic. Even a wound
bleeding at 1 hit/round will cause death within min-
utes. Minor bleeding wounds that clot on their own
are part of generic hit damage taken. Bleeding is sub-
```
tracted from hits at the end of every round (during the
```
```
upkeep phase).
```
bReakage
```
Some criticals will trigger a breakage roll (Section
```
```
6.1) in the location hit, sometimes at a penalty. This
```
could be armor, a shield, or an item carried at that lo-
cation. If the GM decides there are multiple options
for breakage, randomly determine which one of the
possible items in that area requires a breakage roll.
If the critical indicates an item is dropped it must make
an immediate breakage roll. In addition to determining
if the item has been damaged or broken, the ones die
of the breakage roll is how far away the item has land-
ed, in feet. The GM can overrule this if it does not
```
make sense (e.g., an anvil will always land near your
```
```
feet, but a ball may end up far away).
```
fatigUe
Fatigue can represent anything from getting the wind
```
knocked out of you to getting out-maneuvered (there-
```
by requiring additional energy with which to attack
```
or defend). Fatigue penalties are effective immediately
```
```
and can be lowered by normal means (e.g., rest, Abso-
```
```
lute Success on an Endurance roll, spells, etc.).
```
Keep in mind that fatigue results in excess of -50 total
should be converted into an injury, which takes effect
immediately. The specifics of the fatigue injury should
be determined by the GM, based on the critical or
fumble that caused the penalty to increase past -50.
177
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
gRapple
Grapples are often due to unarmed attacks such as
wrestling, but can also occur from nets, bolas, or other
entangling attacks. Grapple results are given as a per-
centage representing how effectively the defender has
been grabbed, held, or entangled. The grapple % is a
penalty to all actions for the defender until he breaks
free. Grappling is cumulative, so a 30% grapple re-
sult followed by a 10% grapple results in the defender
grappled for 40% and making actions at -40.
The defender can attempt to break or slip free of a
grapple using the rules for contested Feats of Strength
```
(Section 5.2) using his Wrestling, Contortions, or
```
other appropriate grappling skill, with the defender
subject to the full grapple penalty. If the grappler is an
object such as a net rather than a combatant, treat the
escape attempt as a Medium maneuver, otherwise use
the opponent’s weight to determine the difficulty. Any
attempt to break free from a grapple is a 2-4 AP action
```
(4 AP at -0, 3 AP at -25, or 2 AP at -50). If grappled
```
by multiple attackers, rolls must be made to escape
from each one, although the total penalty applies to
```
all rolls, and no additional AP are required (a single
```
```
action permits a roll against each attacker). The grap-
```
ple is automatically broken if the attacker is stunned,
incapacitated, or decides to release the defender.
As with other conflicting maneuvers, the amount the
attacker rolls over 100 is subtracted from the target’s
roll to escape and the result resolved as a Percentage
Maneuver. The grapple is reduced by the Percentage
Maneuver result.
```
Example: Aldrik has chased a cultist into a cave,
```
where he is attacked by an entangling vine and the
cultist himself. The vine achieves a 10% grapple
```
and the cultist 20%. Aldrik spends the full round (4
```
```
AP) attempting to break free. Aldrik is a passable
```
wrestler with a +43 Wrestling skill. Since the vine
is a plant, the gamemaster considers it a Medium
```
maneuver to escape (+0). The vine has a grappling
```
OB of +30 and rolls 35 for a total of 65, adding
100 – 65 = 35 to Aldrik’s roll of 63 and his skill for
a total of 141, minus the total grapple amount of
30. The final result of 111 gives a result of 100 on
the percentage maneuver table, so he is free of the
plant.
At the same time, Aldrik is trying to break free from
the cultist. Since they weigh approximately the same
amount, each is trying to control 100% of their own
```
weight, resulting in a Very Hard (-20) maneuver
```
for both combatants. The cultist rolls an 88 with a
skill of 60, minus 20, subtracting 28 from Aldrik’s
new roll. Aldrik’s roll to break free is a roll of 47 +
43 skill -20 for the Very Hard maneuver -28 for his
foe’s roll -30 for the grapple amounts, for a total of
12. This yields a result of 10 on the percentage ma-
neuver table, reducing the cultist’s grip from a 20%
grapple to just 10%, but Aldrik is not free yet.
Until the grapple is broken, the attacker and defender
are bound together, and neither can move away with-
```
out a contested Feat of Strength (Section 5.2) based on
```
Weight-Training skill to move their foe. This Feat is
part of their movement action and does not require any
additional expenditure of AP, but also will not break
```
the grapple; if successful, the foe is dragged along,
```
unless the attacker chooses to let go.
```
Example: Anika, a 210 lbs warrior from the snowy
```
north, is grappled by a 93 lbs hobgoblin, who has
somehow managed to achieve a 40% grapple. She
has better things to do and tries to walk away. The
hobgoblin is 44% of her weight, making it a Medi-
um maneuver for her, while she is 225% of the hob-
```
goblin, putting him at an Absurd (-70) maneuver.
```
The hobgoblin rolls 67 plus his skill of 50, minus 70
for a total of 47, adding 53 to Anika’s roll. Wrestling
isn’t her strongest suit, but she has a +47 skill and
rolls a 61, plus +0 for a Medium maneuver and +53
from the hobgoblin’s roll, minus 40 for the grapple,
for a total of 121, which yields a 100 result on the
percentage maneuver table. The hobgoblin isn’t
even slowing her down.
hits
Hits are most often inflicted from the Attack Table,
however critical effects can also cause hits. A com-
batant is knocked unconscious when he drops to 0 or
fewer hits, but he takes penalties to all actions once
more than 25% of total hits are lost, as shown in Table
9-8b. Death occurs when a character has taken hits
beyond zero that exceed that character’s Body Devel-
```
opment skill bonus. (Thus, a character with a 50 bonus
```
```
in Body Development skill dies at -51 hits.)
```
Table 9-8b: Hit Loss Penalties
Hits Lost Penalty
0 - 25% 0
26 - 50% -10
51 - 75% -20
76% + -30
178
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
penalties
Many injuries result in a penalty, which is applied
to absolute and percentage maneuvers, spellcast-
ing, attacks, and full or partial blocking or dodging.
```
Penalties represent the loss of functionality (whereas
```
pain is represented by hit loss and its corresponding
```
penalties). Penalties from all injuries stack, although if
```
the affected area has nothing to do at all with the ma-
neuver then the penalty should not apply at all. If the
affected area is not being directly used, but does have
some role, then half the penalty may be appropriate.
When in doubt apply the full sum of all penalties. All
injury penalties last until the injury is healed, although
```
the penalty diminishes over time (Section 13.1).
```
```
Example: Flinky’s -10 for a broken finger does not
```
apply to jumping over the pit, and her -20 from
broken toes does not apply to picking a lock on the
door further down the hall. Trying to pull the heavy,
somewhat stuck door open would face the full -30
penalty for both.
The sum of a character’s penalties generally reflects
how broken he is. Penalties of -50 or more mean a
character is having serious problems, and should be
role-played that way. As penalties approach -100 char-
acters are approaching incapacitation.
Once penalties reach -100, the character is in serious
```
distress. Any action that requires effort (movement, at-
```
tacks, spell casting or any maneuvers requiring phys-
```
ical effort) will provoke a fatigue check for the round.
```
This will continue until penalties have been reduced to
a lower severity than -100. You need to sit down and
rest, and any action will tend to make things worse.
At total penalties of -200 or more, the character is
dying from his collective injuries, and must make a
fatigue check every round, even if lying still. This will
continue until total penalties are reduced below -200.
Once penalties reach or exceed -300, the character will
die on the next upkeep phase due to systemic shock.
staggeRed
Staggered means that the combatant has been knocked
off balance. A staggered result immediately loses 1 Ac-
tion Point, either this round if the target has any left,
or the beginning of the following round. A staggered
combatant may still parry, as long as the last Action
```
Point he spent was on a melee action, but his OB (and
```
```
consequently his Parry) may be reduced if fewer Action
```
Points are available to be spent on his attack action. Any
in-progress action may be continued upon the combat-
ant’s next opportunity to expend an Action Point.
```
Example: Cael is firing his bow, starting in the first
```
Action Phase. After spending his second AP in the
second Action Phase, he is struck by a thrown rock
and Staggered. He loses his AP in the third Action
Phase, but is able to complete firing his bow in the
fourth Action Phase, having expended a total of
three AP.
```
Example: Lexxa begins her round with a Perception
```
```
check (2 AP) and spots a foe hiding in the bush-
```
es. On the third Action Phase she begins a melee
attack, but is struck from behind and Staggered. She
loses her fourth AP and is unable to complete the
attack. If she had only spent one AP on Perception
and started her attack in the second Action Phase,
she would have been able to resolve her attack at
the very end of the round, at a penalty of -50 for a 2
AP melee attack.
179
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
pRone
Prone means the target has been thrown flat to the floor
and is subject to the usual modifiers to action for prone
position, and he must take a 2 Action Point action to
```
stand up again (this may be reduced to 1 AP with a suc-
```
```
cessful Acrobatics maneuver).
```
knocked back
Being knocked back will move the character a distance
in feet in a given direction, as indicated by the critical
result. Depending on the terrain the combatant may
suffer additional effects. Being knocked over an edge
or into a stairwell/steep slope may provoke a fall, being
knocked into water or fire may provoke skill checks
or criticals. Being knocked into an object may inflict
damage on the person and/or a breakage check on the
```
object crashed into (depending on their relative sturdi-
```
```
ness). The GM should use his discretion, but even the
```
worst case scenario of being thrown directly into a solid
wall or a rack of spears should not inflict a critical of
a higher severity level than the one that provoked the
```
knockback (use an A if in doubt).
```
Unless the target is knocked unconscious and knocked
back, the GM should allow the knocked back target a
```
maneuver roll (using either the appropriate movement
```
```
skill or Acrobatics) to attempt to catch himself at a dif-
```
ficulty determined by the GM based on the situation.
If the “object” the combatant is knocked back into is
another aware entity, both the combatant and the tar-
get may end up harmed in the collision. If the knocked
back combatant is coming from the front of the second
```
character, assume the target sees him coming; if he
```
comes from the flank or behind, have the target make a
Perception maneuver to see the knocked back charac-
```
ter coming at him (difficulty based on how chaotic the
```
```
situation is). The GM should determine the difficulty
```
of getting out of the way based on the specifics of the
situation and make a maneuver using Acrobatics or
the appropriate Movement skill to get out of the way.
Location is fuzzy enough within “combat radius” that
this maneuver should be considered an instantaneous
action. If they fail to move out of the way, they are
knocked prone.
stUn
A stun means the combatant has been knocked hard
enough to disorient and put him in a vulnerable state.
The effect is a temporary penalty each round, either
-25, -50, or -75, as indicated in the critical, representing
different amounts of disorientation:
•[-25]: A minor disorientation where the combatant is
dizzy and unstable.
•[-50]: The combatant has been hit seriously enough
to disrupt his senses, and he is struggling to control
his body.
•[-75]: Suffering from a serious blow, the combatant is
having a hard time remaining standing and nearly
at his foe’s mercy.
All stun results are cumulative, with the total rounds of
Stun[-25] adding together, Stun[-50] adding together,
and so on. One round of stun effect wears off at the
```
end of the round, from the worst effect to the least (-75
```
```
rounds wear off first, then -50, then -25).
```
```
Example: A few rounds into a battle, Taor has 2
```
rounds of Stun[-50] and 1 round of Stun[-25]. Un-
fortunately he then gets hit from behind, resulting
in one round of Stun[-75] and 1 round of Stun[-25].
He now will be at -75 for 1 round, then -50 for two
rounds, then -25 for 2 rounds. Poor Taor!
Stunned characters are in a state of mental confusion
due to a blow, and players should be encouraged to role-
play this state. Any maneuvers related to memories of
events that happened during a stunned round should be
```
made at -50. This is true even years later; the character
```
simply lacks a clear memory of those events.
```
Example: During the fight, Elsarde was stunned
```
when the priest shouted a threat at the group of PCs
attacking the temple. After the fight, Elsarde asks
the GM what the priest was shouting. The GM has
her make a Perception maneuver to see if she was
paying attention to such details, which is at -50
because she was stunned. Alas, she fails: “I dun-
no Victor, that whole middle of the fight is kind of
hazy.”
```
Some creatures may be immune to stun (as noted in the
```
```
creature description), however they are generally not
```
immune to the knocking around that comes with a good
stun. Instead, the target suffers a flat -25 for the remain-
```
der of the round (regardless of the severity of stun), or
```
the following round if he has no Action Points left to
spend. The penalty does not stack with additional stuns,
it can only be a -25 for 1 round.
```
Example: Borin attacks a Skeleton as it comes
```
charging at him and rolls a critical that deals Stun[-
75] for 5 rounds. The Skeleton, being immune to
stuns, is knocked off-balance and is at -25 for the rest
of the round and will suffer no penalty thereafter.180
