UNIFIED

Arms Law
9.9. Extended Example
Kirax and Thyrzia have just started work as bounty
```
hunters (see their stats in the examples of Chapter
```
```
2). They are on the tail of Harshak, a notorious goat
```
rustler. It's the first bounty they're trying to pick
up as a team, so it's not exactly the most glamor-
ous, but hopefully it will lead to something bigger.
They've tracked Harshak to a camp in the woods,
where they see Harshak and his partners Gavart
and Caitsley are getting ready to raid a nearby
homestead for livestock.
Kirax uses his Survival skill to make an educated
guess which route they will take, rolling a 77 plus
his skill of 39 for a total of 116, success. Then he
and Thyrzia use Stalking to lay an ambush, with one
of them on each side of the trail. The goat rustlers
fail to notice them during their initial approach, but
the gamemaster gives them another chance during
the ambush. Stalking is resolved as a conflicting
skill. Kirax rolls 45 plus his skill of 43, for a total of
88. This results in a bonus of 12 to the Perception
rolls of those attempting to notice him. Thyrzia does
better, rolling 80 plus her skill of 29 for 109, which
will subtract 9 from those Perception rolls. Gavart,
in the lead, rolls 28 plus his skill of 32, and notices
neither bounty hunter. Harshak, in the rear, rolls
```
36 plus his skill of 40; he gets a partial success to
```
notice Kirax but completely fails to notice Thyrzia.
```
Caitsley, in the middle, rolls 77 with her skill of 30;
```
she notices Kirax and thinks there might be some-
one else in the area.
At this point the gamemaster moves to combat
rounds as conflict is imminent. The first round starts
with Gavart in striking range of Thyrzia and Cait-
sley in range of Kirax, with Harshak further back
on the trail. Initiative is Thyrzia first with 23, then
```
Caitsley (13), Harshak (10), Kirax (9), and Gavart
```
```
(3). Thyrzia and Kirax are within 10' of their targets
```
but do not know the outcome of their quarry’s
perception rolls. On the first phase, they each begin
attacks, with Thyrzia moving to Gavart and Kirax
```
moving to Caitsley. Moving at a Creep pace (with
```
```
no pace penalty), they will reach their foes by the
```
end of the round. At this point, they notice none of
their foes have reacted to them, so they have at least
achieved surprise, although they do not yet know to
what degree.
On the second phase, Kirax and Thyrzia each have
```
the option to resolve a quick attack (2 AP) at -50,
```
hitting before their foes have any chance to react, or
they can continue a full attack and hope they have
not been spotted. They decide to have faith in their
plan and continue the attack. On the third phase,
Thyrzia continues her attack against Gavart. Caits-
ley, who was surprised but not flat-footed, can now
act. She draws a broadsword and makes a run for it,
```
moving 15' (a Run pace). She does not take a defen-
```
```
sive action (e.g. making an attack so she can parry,
```
```
or a partial dodge), because the -75 pace penalty
```
would render those actions moot based on her skill
```
bonuses (since her OB is +55 and her Running skill,
```
```
for dodging, is +40).
```
Kirax has another choice. Caitsley was in his melee
range, due to the reach of his two-handed spear, so
he can either continue his full 4 AP attack pursuing
```
at a run (with a -75 pace penalty), or he can take
```
```
his attack now (at -25 for a 3 AP attack). Either
```
```
way, it will be a rear attack (+35), but he's lost his
```
chance for a Surprise bonus or to use his Ambush
```
skill; for those, he would have needed to attack in
```
the 2nd phase before she reacted. Kirax decides to
attack now. The attack occurs on Caitsley's ini-
tiative, but uses Kirax's action point for the third
```
phase. The total modifiers are +60 (his skill) +10
```
```
(for a two-handed weapon) +35 (for a rear attack)
```
```
-25 (for a 3 AP attack), totaling +80. He could
```
reserve part of this bonus for parrying, but with
surprised foes, he decides that's unnecessary. His
attack roll is 59 for a total of 139. Caitsley is wear-
```
ing AT3, and this is a Big attack (because the spear
```
```
is two-handed), so the result is 23 D Puncture.
```
He rolls a 44 for the critical, a thrust to the knee
damaging the joint and causing serious bleeding.
The effect is bleeding 4 hits per round, a -5 injury
penalty, a -10 fatigue penalty, and two rounds of
Stun[ -25]. Caitsley stumbles away in serious pain.
Harshak, with a partial success on his perception
roll, has an idea something is going on. He spends
the third phase making a 1 AP perception roll to fig-
ure out exactly what, and easily notices Kirax now
that the attack has begun.
Kirax would be next in the initiative, but he already
used his action for the phase against Caitsley. Ga-
vart is last, and still flat-footed and oblivious. He'll
be able to act next round.
181
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
On the fourth phase, Thyrzia resolves her attack
against Gavart. She's armed with a light mace and
a hand axe. She opts to attack with both. The base
penalty for two attacks is -75, but since both weap-
ons are light, it is reduced to -55, and her Multiple
```
Attacks skill of 20 reduces it to -35. (She’s added
```
this up in advance, since it is a tactic she plans to
```
use whenever she has the advantage.) She also has
```
the ambidextrous talent, eliminating any off-hand
penalty. With Gavart flat-footed, he has no defen-
sive bonus, and she gains a bonus of +35 for a rear
attack and +25 for surprise. Her total bonus based
on her OB of +39 is +64. She considers making a
subduing attack, but that's one too many penalties
and she decides against. She rolls a 41 for the first
attack for 105, and an 86 on the second for 150.
Gavart is in AT3, and her attacks are Small because
they are light weapons, so the mace scores a 7AK
and the hand axe 16DS. For the Krush critical,
she rolls a 55 and adds her Ambush ranks for a
total of 59, cracking a bone in his hand for 14 hits,
bleeding 1 hit per round, and a -5 injury penalty.
The Slashing critical is a 58, modified to 62, hitting
Gavart in the elbow for 32 more hits, a -15 penalty,
and a round of Stun[-25]. Gavart has taken a total
of 69 damage. That's more than he has, so he falls
unconscious. If the bleeding is not stopped, he will
eventually die.
Caitsley has the next initiative and keeps running.
```
The gamemaster rules the forest is Hard (-10) ter-
```
rain to move in. Caitsley is pretty spry, and normal-
ly a roll might not be needed, but now she has a -5
injury penalty to one leg, a -10 fatigue penalty, and
is Stunned[-25]. He rolls to see how she does, roll-
ing 02 for an open-ended down result, followed by
an 8. Her Running skill is +40, so the final total is
-6, resolved as a percentage maneuver. She stumbles
and fails to get anywhere, but manages not to hurt
herself any further.
Harshak unslings a spear and starts backing up.
Kirax advances on Harshak. With only one action
phase left, he can't start an attack, but he can at
least close the distance. Gavart gurgles a bit.
At the end of the round, Gavart loses a hit due to
bleeding, and Caitsley loses four. Caitsley is still
stunned, since she had spent 3 action points before
being stunned last round.
```
In the second round, Thyrzia is first again (16), then
```
```
Harshak (12) and Kirax are tied (12), and Caitsley
```
```
is last (1) in part due to -3 from her various penal-
```
ties.
Thyrzia moves towards Harshak. Rather than
closing, she decides to throw her hand axe. Harshak
and Kirax both begin attacks against each other.
Caitsley swears and tries to get a bandage around
```
her bleeding wound (using direct pressure). Thyr-
```
zia's action will be resolved in the third phase, and
everyone else in the fourth phase, so the gamemas-
```
ter skips forward. Thyrzia throws her axe; 32 plus
```
her skill of 38 for a 70. It whizzes by Harshak's
head, barely missing him.
In the fourth phase, Thyrzia draws another hand
axe, and Kirax and Harshak make simultaneous
attacks against each other. Kirax applies 15 OB to
his parry, while Harshak makes a reckless all-out
attack. Kirax rolls a 95 for 153, while Harshak
rolls a 66 for 111. Kirax deals a 26EP and Harshak
a 17CP. Kirax's critical is a 97, tearing through
Harshak's chest and guaranteeing a slow, painful
```
death (and immediate incapacitation). Harshak
```
rolls a 62, stabbing Kirax in the shoulder for 14
hits, a -10 injury penalty, and bleeding 4 hits per
round.
Also in the fourth phase, Caitsley succeeds at a
Medicine maneuver to stop her bleeding, but it will
take four minutes of treatment to keep from immedi-
ately opening up on her. She calls out, "I... surren-
der?" Kirax, with his own injury, is happy to accept
that and let her go. The bounty was on Harshak. It
would have paid better if he was alive, but, well, not
much to be done about that now.

Arms Law
Chapter 10:Chapter 10:
Attack TablesAttack Tables
183
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Arms Law
Tables for each type of attack are given on their own
page, including typical modifiers and statistics for the
attack as well as optional Small and Big versions of
the attack table.
For each attack type, the following information is list-
```
ed:
```
•Criticals: The types of criticals that may be inflicted
by the attack, including the letter used to denote
the critical type on the attack tables.
•Disarm modifier: The penalty to Disarm attempts,
from Table 9-7a in Section 9.7.
•Subdual modifier: The penalty to Subdual attempts,
from Table 9-7e in Section 9.7.
•Size: For weapons and natural attacks, the size is
given as a modifier. A +0 size is an attack of the
```
same size as the attacker (typically Medium). A
```
+1 size is an attack one size larger, e.g., a Medi-
um attacker with a +1 size attack will make a Big
attack. For spells, the size is given as the actual
```
attack size (e.g., Medium), as the size of the spell
```
is independent of the size of the caster.
•Length: The length of a typical weapon. This also
```
defines the wielder’s melee range (half the char-
```
```
acter’s height plus the length of the weapon). For
```
weapons other than Medium size, multiply the
length by 1.6 for each size larger, or divide by 1.6
for each size smaller.
•Strength: The strength of a typical weapon as ap-
```
plied to breakage checks (Section 6.1).
```
•Weight: The weight of a typical weapon. For weap-
ons other than Medium size, multiply the weight
by 4 for each size larger, or divide by 4 for each
size smaller.
•Fumble: Any unmodified roll between 1 and the
Fumble number results in a fumble. The fumble
number is reduced by 1 for every 5 ranks the
character has in their applicable attack skill, to a
minimum of 1.
•Range: For ranged attacks, the maximum range for
each range increment. No modifier applies for
attacks up to the listed Short range, a -10 modi-
fier applies for attacks beyond Short range up to
Medium range, etc. Crossbows also have a Point
Blank range within which they gain a +20 bonus.
Optionally, range may be adjusted based on the
```
attack size (see Section 9.4).
```
small and big tables
The optional Small and Big tables incorporate both
```
a critical severity adjustment (one level, appropriate
```
```
for Small or Big attacks against Medium targets) and
```
```
an adjustment to the number of hits delivered (x0.75
```
```
for Small and x1.5 for Big). If the adjustment to hits
```
delivered is not desired, use only the Medium tables.
If using the optional hit adjustments for larger and
smaller attacks, for attacks smaller than Small or larg-
er than Big use the Medium table and multiply the hits
delivered by the factor on Table 9-4a.
If the target of the attack is not Medium, modify the
critical severity according to the target size as shown
on Table 9-4a. For example, if an attack is made
against a Big target, reduce the critical severity by one
level. The Small and Big tables already incorporate
the adjustment for the attack size.
Due to the critical severity adjustment for size, the
Small tables include criticals of less than A severity.
These are denoted as Z. Roll an A critical, but find the
next lower result that affects the same area of the body
on the A column and apply it.

X: Bleeding, at X hits every round

```
(-X): Fatigue penalty of X
```
```
 or (-X): Breakage roll at –X (if given)
```
```
-X or T(-X): Injury penalty of X for T time period
```
T  [-25/-50/-75]: Stunned at the given penalty for
T rounds or timespan.
```
 or  (X) : Staggered (loses 1 AP or X, if given)
```
 X': Knocked back distance of X'
: Knocked prone/pushed to ground
 X% : Grappled X%
💀💀: Target is dead, dying, or otherwise defeated
```
(unable or unwilling to fight)
```
```
(X): Additional criticals of X severity
```
cRitical tables
Some critical tables represent common types of weap-
on strikes, some represent special types of attack, and
others describe the effects of different types of energy
or magic. The following critical tables are provided:
•Acid: Caustic or acidic attacks inflict acid criticals.
This may be from enchanted creatures that secrete
or expel acid as a defense mechanism.
•Cold: Elemental cold attacks such as from spells or
magical effects inflict cold criticals, and extreme
```
cold conditions may do so as well. (The abbrevia-
```
tion for Cold criticals on the attack tables is O, to
```
avoid confusion with C severity criticals.)
```
•Electricity: Most often from enchanted attacks or
creatures, lightning criticals could also be inflicted
```
from electrical devices or natural lightning. (The
```
abbreviation for Electricity criticals is L, to avoid
```
confusion with E severity criticals.)
```
•Grapple: Grapple criticals are usually inflicted by
unarmed attacks that focus on grabbing and hold-
ing. They may also come from some weapons
such as whips.
•Heat: Natural extreme fire and heat, as well as en-
chanted elemental heat attacks such as fire balls,
inflict heat criticals.
•Holy: Weapons or spells from a divine source, such
as from a godly being or from someone channeling
the power of a godly being, may cause holy crit-
icals. Holy weapons usually inflict holy criticals
in addition to the normal type from the weapon
itself.
•Impact: Blunt force that covers large areas of the
body, such as a falling block, a fall, or ram attack,
cause impact criticals.
•Krush: Blunt crushing weapons like maces and
```
clubs cause krush criticals. (Krush criticals are
```
abbreviated as K on the attack tables to avoid con-
```
fusion with C severity criticals.)
```
•Puncture: Piercing attacks come from pointed
weapons like arrows, stabbing blades like dag-
gers, or from natural weaponry like stingers.
•Slash: Slashing criticals involve a sharp edge cut-
ting the target, and are most often used by blades
such as swords and axes.
•Steam: Heated water vapor can be scalding and in-
flict serious burns, from either natural or enchant-
ed sources.
•Strikes: Unarmed attacks that focus on punching
and kicking are like crushing attacks causing
bruising, stuns, and concussion damage, but rarely
breaking the skin except with the most serious of
```
blows. (Unarmed Strikes criticals are abbreviated
```
```
as T on the attack tables.)
```
•Sweeps: Unarmed attacks primarily using throws
and sweeping attacks are meant to redirect and
use the foe’s weight against him. These attacks
unbalance the foe and can also cause serious blunt
force damage through dropping the foe or impacts
with objects or walls.
•Subdual: While subdual attacks are always meant
to be non-lethal, death is still a possibility, as is
serious permanent injury. Most injuries however
tend to be temporary, such as fatigue, stunning, or
short duration penalties.
•Unbalance: Severe hits from melee attacks often
will cause secondary criticals in addition to the
normal critical type. The force behind these blows
can seriously unbalance the foe causing him to fall
or otherwise injure himself.
11. Critic al Tables11. Critic al Tables

243
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster Law
Character Law provides the roles to be
played as well as the skills and the ma-
neuvers to use them in. Arms Law offers
the characters the challenge of battle, with
its risks and dangers. Here, Gamemaster
Law provides the remaining tools a game-
master will need to create their stories and
adventures.
Chapter 12 provides the tools for design-
ing a campaign, defining the tone and
power level of the game, and customizing
the races, cultures, and professions to suit
the setting. Guidelines are provided for ex-
perience points, to reward players for ac-
complishing tasks and advancing the story.
Chapter 13 covers health, healing, and
death, including the treatment of injuries
and their natural recovery.
Part III:Part III:
Gamemaster LawGamemaster Law
In Chapter 14, the psychology and inter-
actions of non-player characters are de-
scribed, providing guidance in determining
their reactions. Players may need to per-
suade, inspire, or deceive them to achieve
their goals, depending on whether they are
allies, adversaries, or simply inhabitants of
the world the Gamemaster is creating.
The final chapter describes hazards of the
environment. These include extremes of
temperature, drowning, falling, poison,
disease, and darkness.
Being the Gamemaster of a roleplaying
game involves a lot of separate jobs rolled
up into one. The GM must direct the action,
act out all the non-player roles, resolve
disputes, and keep the flow of the game
moving. Stepping back from the action,
the GM also writes all the elements of the
story not controlled by the players. Role-
master can be used as the basis for many
different stories, and the rules in these sec-
tions will help the GM get the job done at
the gaming table and customize the game
to fit the style of action he wants to run.
244
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster Law
12.1. Power Level
Rolemaster is easily customizable to allow for a wide
variety of gaming styles and settings. Some groups
may like to play low-powered games where the char-
acters are ordinary, while others prefer games where
their characters are epic figures in a dynamic world.
Some skills, spells, or talents may not be appropriate
in one campaign, but necessary or even casual ele-
ments in another. To allow for a broad range of power,
Rolemaster can be scaled based on the relative power
of the characters to the rest of the world.
The power level of the characters may range from
Average to Epic. These power levels are used as a
guideline in making decisions about what rules are to
be allowed. The GM may even decide to use different
power levels in different parts of the rules, such as
using a Heroic power level for generating characters
but an Average power level when dealing with spell
```
list availability (Spell Law) or power point recovery.
```
These power levels are given to help GMs, not to tie
them down or hamper them, so as always the GM can
modify as he sees fit.
•Average – The player characters are average people.
Training in skills will be the single most import-
ant factor in play, rather than exceptional stats or
innate talents.
•Superior – The player characters are well above
average. Training in skills is still a very strong
factor, but they will stand out as better than peo-
ple of the same profession and level due to their
```
individual superiority and natural talent. (This is
```
```
Rolemaster’s default level).
```
•Heroic – The player characters are highly excep-
tional, in the top 1% of the population. While
training in skills is still important, the characters
are all cream of the crop, Olympic quality level
```
in their chosen fields; their innate ability makes
```
them stand out above the average compared to
other characters of the same profession and level.
12. Ga mem astering12. Ga mem astering
•Legendary – The player characters at this pow-
er level are now once in a generation prodigies.
Training in skills gets a giant push from innate
talent. They now not only stand out in the crowd,
the crowd has a hard time keeping up.
•Epic – The player characters at epic power levels
exceed the averages by so much they no longer
really fit into the same frame. They are pushing
into superhuman or demigod-like levels of innate
ability.
The most obvious effect of varying Power Level is
in the character’s statistics, which has an impact on
all levels. Varying the total bonuses available for pro-
fessional knacks will have the biggest impact on low
level characters.
Stat boosts refers to the option to replace a stat with
average values, replace your highest temporary stat
with 90, etc., as described in Section 2.5. When re-
placing a stat value with average values, see Table
12-1 for those values. For example, at the Heroic lev-
el, the average temporary stat is 61, and the average
potential is 81.
For power point recovery, characters get back the list-
ed percentage of their Power Development total every
two hours of sleep, as indicated in Table 12-1. This
will have an effect on magic-using classes at all levels
by increasing how many spells they will ultimately
be able to cast over time. The maximum recovery per
day is achieved in eight hours of sleep. Characters that
need less sleep will recover more quickly but to the
same maximum. For example, a character that needs 4
hours of sleep per night will recover the listed percent-
age each hour of sleep but only to a limit of 4 hours.
```
Example: Shadow World is a high fantasy setting
```
with potentially powerful foes and magic. The GM
wants the characters to be heroes in this setting
where they use powerful magic and weapons to help
combat epic foes and so sets the campaign’s Power
Level to Legendary.
Table 12-1: Power Level
Professional
Knacks
PP Recovery
per 2 hr sleep
Reroll Stats
Under
Average Stats Stat Stat
Power Temp. Pot. Boosts Points
Average 0 5% - 51 76 1 5
```
Superior + 10 (+5 max) 10% 11 56 78 2 15
```
```
Heroic + 20 (+10 max) 15% 21 61 81 3 25
```
```
Legendary + 30 (+15 max) 20% 31 66 83 4 35
```
```
Epic + 40 (+20 max) 25% 41 71 86 5 45245
```
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster Law
```
Example: A game set in Europe’s Dark Ages has no
```
magic at all, and with limited technology. The GM
begins the characters as normal peasants, with no
special powers and of average abilities. He sets the
campaign’s Power Level to Average.
chaRacteRs
Some GMs and players prefer stronger or weaker
starting characters for different styles of game play.
```
The default Superior level method (Section 2.5) will
```
generate slightly above average characters.
Superior characters will have average temporary stats
of 56 and average potential stats of 78. To use a dif-
ferent power method, change the Reroll threshold. For
Superior anything 10 or less is rolled again, while for
Average power level there are no rerolls. The ‘Average
Stat’ columns on table 12-1 indicate what the average
stats would be for different power levels. In addition,
players may replace their rolled temporary and poten-
tial stats for two stats with the averages shown on the
```
table (56 and 78 for Superior characters).
```
If using the optional point-buy method, change the
number of points used to buy stats as shown in Table
12-1. The default method presented in Section 3.5
gives 15 points.
Power level also determines the bonuses given as Pro-
fessional Knacks. The default power level presented
in Section 2.4 gives two +5 bonuses. Use Table 12-1 to
determine Professional Knacks for other power levels.
```
The notation of “+10 (+5 max)” means a total of +10
```
bonus, but a max of +5 to any one skill.
12.2. Starting Level
Varying the starting level permits very different styles
of play. Some GMs may want to run a group of inex-
perienced waifs through a city adventure while other
GMs want to run experienced heroes fighting power-
ful foes. Level is different than Power Level. Power
Level represents the character's overall power relative
to his peers, while level is a gauge of experience.
Rolemaster is primarily a skill-based game, which
assumes a certain amount of training and real-life ex-
perience. While age does not solely determine real-life
experience, it is true that older characters tend to be
more experienced and thus higher level. However,
there can certainly be exceptionally well-traveled
teenagers and stagnant adults. Individuals who have
worked their way through difficult circumstances are
the most likely to have advanced in level. Those who
have lived a static, familiar routine have less opportu-
nity to grow and develop. Individuals driven by ambi-
tion, curiosity, or a desire to improve themselves may
exceed these expectations.
When deciding starting level, consider the types and
difficulties of the challenges in the campaign, as well
as the background of the characters that are to be part
of the story. A first level character is not a veteran of
many battles, nor is a tenth level character a naïve
farm boy.
The first few levels in Rolemaster are often made up
of adolescents who are still apprenticed, depending on
the profession. Table 12-2 has examples of names or
titles based on level and an age guide, indicating level
for one’s approximate age. The GM may find this more
useful for NPCs, keeping in mind that actual level can
vary wildly depending on the type of NPC desired.
These are not rules written in stone, merely guidelines
from which a GM can start from before they tweak to
fit their world or any specific character or NPC.
First level characters have minimal training and little
experience. They may be apprentices or novices who
are beginning their studies but have not yet reached
any advanced level of competence. Spellcasters will
have few spells, in some cases only permitting them
a support role. Adventures at this level may focus on
earning their apprenticeships or serve as a prologue
to a story that is only beginning to unfold. Challenges
based on skills should allow for the possibility of fail-
ure, with easier tasks or tasks which can be complet-
```
ed gradually over multiple attempts (e.g. percentage
```
```
maneuvers). Competition against their peers provides
```
many opportunities to win and lose. Combat should
be low stakes, with foes who are more interested in
scaring them off, teaching them a lesson, or robbing
them, rather than fights to the death. Avoid outnum-
bering a first level party, as a numerical advantage can
quickly become overwhelming. Foes at this level are
likely to also be untrained, and may break and flee as
soon as a fight turns against them. Fights with more
serious consequences should be tilted in favor of the
PCs, with a numerical advantage or superior position
such as an unwary enemy. If you want to run a cam-
paign focused on young, unusually talented prodigies
up against more serious threats, consider increasing
the power level, as higher stats and knacks will to
some extent compensate for few skill ranks, although
spellcasters will still be severely limited much more
than their martial companions.
Table 12-2: Levels
Level Names Typical Age
1 Novice, Assistant, Page Pre-teenager
2 Jr Apprentice, Squire Teenager
3 Apprentice, Knight-in-waiting Young Adult
4 Sr Apprentice, Man-at-arms, Scholar Adult
5+ Journeyman, Knight, Sr Scholar Adult246
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster Law
Second and third level characters are reaching a ba-
sic level of competence. As adventurers go, they
are still beginners but have more ability to stand on
their own. Spellcasters will have a reasonable but not
overwhelming variety of spells, some of which will
be more potent especially if they have developed lists
above their level. Some of the same considerations ap-
ply as for first level characters, but it's not as necessary
to go easy on them, especially once the players are
familiar with their characters' abilities. They won't be
the most capable people around, but might be called
upon when the more experienced NPCs already have
their hands full.
For players who are unfamiliar with Rolemaster,
starting at second level can provide a good balance,
allowing their characters to be reasonably capable but
not overwhelming the players with too many options.
Their characters can grow as their familiarity with
the system advances, giving them more opportunity
to adjust their character development based on their
experience.
By fifth level, the characters are skilled and capable.
In many cases, especially in smaller settlements, they
may be the best suited to deal with problems and dan-
gers, and people may turn to them for help. This is es-
pecially true if they are spellcasters in settings where
magic is uncommon. Whatever their professions, they
can handle more serious threats and have more ability
to come up with unexpected solutions to problems,
although there will still be situations they are better
off running from. Required maneuvers can be more
difficult and fights can be higher stakes, although most
intelligent foes regardless of level are prone to run or
surrender when the battle turns significantly against
them. If the characters are starting at this level, ask
where they received their training and what experienc-
es they have had. Mentors may provide useful con-
tacts, advice, and story hooks.
Tenth level characters are extremely skilled veterans.
They will usually be the most talented people avail-
able and they will be sought out to solve problems and
provide guidance. They can take on serious threats
and, if given the opportunity to plan and gain a strate-
gic advantage while working as a team, may be able to
defeat foes stronger than themselves. If the characters
are starting at this level, they are likely veterans of
extended campaigns. Unless they have traveled from
afar, they will already have reputations and contacts.
Twentieth level characters are legendary. If they
are spellcasters, they are capable of miracles. Their
breadth of abilities will make them harder to chal-
lenge, but there will be no doubt they are the people
to go to when problems arise that are worth their time.
People will travel great distances to seek their help
or apprentice under them, and if they have made any
effort to ingratiate themselves with the local nobility
they are likely to have earned titles and station. Char-
acters starting at this level may be nobles, guildmas-
ters, or high priests, who are forced to set aside their
normal day-to-day duties to deal with unprecedented
threats. They will be well known unless they are new
to the area, in which case they will quickly develop
a mystique and be the focus of wild rumors for good
and ill.
12.3. Starting Wealth
A Gamemaster must also decide how much equipment
and money characters begin with. Take into account
the overall wealth of the setting first, before deter-
mining the character's standing. In some settings only
members of the nobility might be rich, while in some
dark, gritty settings everyone is poor. Do not allow
your players to start the game with 10 gold each if
the royal treasury only contains 500 gold. Table 6-0b
presents starting money based on starting level and
five wealth categories.
In addition to wealth, the GM may place restrictions
on what equipment is available based on the setting as
discussed in Chapter 6. Perhaps no one has invented
plate armor, or civilians may not be allowed to own
swords. If metal is rare you could make any metal
items cost twice as much when players choose starting
equipment.
12.4. Awarding
Experience
Character rewards can take many forms, from treasure
to earning a place in history. However, among these,
```
Experience Points (EP) are one of the most obvious
```
and are a significant factor in most role-playing games.
Characters gain experience, and therefore skills and
abilities, by being awarded EP by the GM at the end of
each playing session.
A character’s level increases every 10,000 EP. This
```
includes Level 1 (i.e., at the start of level 1, a charac-
```
```
ter has 10,000 EP). Thus level can be determined by
```
dividing EP by 10,000 and rounding down.
```
Example: Dral, after a series of adventures, has a to-
```
tal of 75,614 experience points, which is more than
the 70,000 minimum for 7th level and fewer than the
80,000 required to be 8th level. Dral is 7th level.
The system presented here awards EP on the basis of
participating in significant events. This could be per-
sonal accomplishment of set goals, or completing mis-
sions important to the session or overall story. Events247
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster Law
are broken into three types: personal events, session
events, and story events. If more than one event type
appears to apply in a situation, both types of EP will be
awarded. This often results in a single character get-
ting a personal reward on top of the session or story
award that the whole party will get. This makes sure
that people who do more get a bit more reward, but
also ensures that those that support an action and make
it possible also get a fair share. Often failure teaches
us important lessons, so the GM may award experi-
ence for failures that teach a lesson as well.
```
Example: Karlo strikes down the dread demon lord
```
Goraili, a major personal event, but Goraili was
the major opponent of this session, so killing him
was also a major session event. Goraili was one of
the four demon lords the party has been hunting for
months, as part of their plan to eliminate the demon
invasion, so killing Goraili was also a moderate sto-
ry event. While each member of the party receives
EP for accomplishing a major session event and
a moderate story event, only Karlo gets the major
personal event EP for killing the demon lord.
Slower or Faster Advancement
On average, characters will go up a level every 2
or 3 sessions. This may be too slow for some GMs
and too fast for others. For slower leveling, figure
EP using the guidelines below and cut them in half.
For faster leveling double the awards, although it is
recommended that more than 1 level is never gained
at one time.
Some GMs may even want characters to go up the
first few levels quickly and higher levels much more
slowly. In this case the GM may use faster leveling
for levels 1-5, normal leveling for levels 6-20, and
slower leveling for levels 21+.
peRsonal events
A personal event is any event that fulfills the personal
```
plans or desires of the character accomplishing it (or
```
```
being affected by it). If Alex is playing a Bard who
```
seeks out folk tales, learning a new folk tale would be
a personal event, whereas if he kills some Orc simply
to survive, that's an achievement that is in no way per-
sonal, but quite generic and relevant only as a means
to achieving something else.
A minor personal event award should be given for
achieving one of the character's desires without a spe-
```
cific plan (e.g., Kyle the Dragonwatcher sees a dragon
```
```
fly directly overhead for the first time). A moderate
```
personal event should be given for something where
the character was specifically working to achieve that
```
desire when it happened (e.g., Kyle had learned the
```
```
dragon's flight route and waited in a suitable spot). A
```
major personal event should be given for something
that has required longer-range planning and effort to
```
achieve (e.g., Kyle has spent many months to seek out
```
the great dragon Silverflame, researched and brought
an appropriate gift to be allowed into the dragon's
presence, and made a sufficiently favorable impres-
```
sion to be granted an extended audience).
```
On the failure end, if the PC roleplays his disappoint-
ment with whatever poor decision or ill luck resulted
in the poor result sufficiently well, and the GM deems
it worthy, the event may be a learning experience.
Minor Personal Event: 10-100 EP
Moderate Personal Event: 100-500 EP
Major Personal Event: 500-1,000 EP
248
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster Law
session events
Session events are achievements during play that
moved the session forward. These consist of short term
events of the campaign contained within the session
```
(e.g., “found the Orc camp”, “killed all the Orcs in
```
```
the camp,” or “rescued the prisoners from the Orcs”).
```
These events will often be common to everyone in the
group, and so every member of the party will get the
```
full EP awarded (it is not split between them). Each
```
session should include at least one of these events, but
not more than a handful.
Any accomplishment that contributes significantly to
the success of the session is at least a minor event.
To be a moderate session event, it must be something
where failure would make accomplishing the major
event of the session far more difficult or impossible
```
(e.g., defeating the Orc guards and getting over the
```
rope bridge before the Orcs can cut the bridge qual-
ifies because if the bridge were cut, the party would
find it very difficult or impossible to complete their
```
mission). A major session event represents the entire
```
```
point of the session (e.g., did the characters defeat the
```
```
Orcs they had come to fight, or did they fail?). Because
```
various gaming groups may run longer or shorter ses-
sions, consider a “Session” to be roughly equal to a
full chapter of a book if the campaign were a novel.
For failures, if they advance the story, they may still be
considered session events, but the PCs must roleplay
sufficiently well that they have learned from the situa-
tion and/or have tried their hardest despite odds not in
their favor. Losing the town to a massive Orc horde,
but putting up an epic defense before being forced to
withdraw, should not be punished, despite the fact it
advances the situation in a bad way.
Minor Session Event: 100-500 EP
Moderate Session Event: 500-1,000 EP
Major Session Event: 1,000-5,000 EP
stoRy events
Story events are major, long term events of the cam-
paign and advance the major plot line of the campaign
```
(e.g., “cleared all the Orcs west of the river”, “discov-
```
ered the Orcs are being paid to raid by the Temple of
the Black Hand”, or “located the site of the Temple
```
of the Black Hand”). These events are almost always
```
common to everyone in the group, and so every mem-
```
ber of the party will get the full EP reward (it is not
```
```
split between them). Except in sessions where major
```
events come to a climax, there should rarely be more
than one of these types of events accomplished in a
single session. Some sessions will not accomplish a
significant story event at all.
These relate to the larger events in the campaign. Any
session that results in some movement forward in the
overall campaign can be considered a minor story
event. To be a moderate story event, the characters
must have made some significant progress in the over-
all campaign. Major story events are ones that drasti-
cally shift the overall campaign progress. Each session
may have minor impact on the overall story progress,
but climactic sessions may end with moderate story
events accomplished. Major story events are turning
points, usually the focus and result of many sessions
of effort finally coming to a climax.
Sometimes the most epic events are not in your favor,
and failing to stop the ritual that allows the demon lord
to physically enter the world might bode poorly for
future events, but if the PCs did their best, and man-
aged to do as much damage as they could before being
forced to flee, this should still be considered a story
event for EP purposes. The story has advanced in a
```
dark way; you must have just completed part two of
```
a trilogy.
Minor Story Event: 500-1,000 EP
Moderate Story Event: 1,000-5,000 EP
Major Story Event: 5,000-10,000 EP
Development Points as Experience
Rather than gaining experience points and spend-
ing 60 Development Points when a new level is
achieved, EP may be forgone completely and DP
directly awarded. This represents characters contin-
ually learning and improving rather than improving
many skills all at one time and suddenly gaining a
bunch of new spells or abilities.
The guidelines above are still used, except that about
150 EP is equal to 1 DP. Personal events range from
1-6 DP, Session events from 1-30 DP, and Story
events from 3-60 DP, depending on if the event is
minor, moderate, or major. If the slower or faster
leveling option is used, these would be halved or
doubled.
The character is able to use awarded DP immediate-
ly, although the player may choose to save them to
spend on a more expensive skill or talent. The char-
```
acter must still heed leveling restrictions (e.g., if a
```
character takes 1 rank in his weapon of choice and
gains DP later on he must pay the rapid development
cost for a 2nd rank unless he has gone up a new lev-
```
el). The character is considered to have gone up a
```
level once he has been awarded 60 DP. At this point,
he may increase stats.
249
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster Law
12.5. Creating Races
```
Races are balanced by assigning costs (in terms of De-
```
```
velopment Points) for advantages and disadvantages
```
they may have. These are collectively called racial
talents and flaws, and work the same as other talents
```
(Chapter 4).
```
To create a new race, total the DP costs of the race’s
talents & flaws then subtract from 50 to get the Bonus
DP for the Race. Talents need not be limited to those
presented as racial talents. Any talents could poten-
tially be used in creating new races. For instance, all
individuals of your new race may be naturally Ambi-
dextrous.
Race creation is an element of worldbuilding and usu-
ally done by the GM. If a player wants to create a race,
he is going to need to make sure that the GM is okay
with the player adding such an element to the GM’s
game world.
```
Example: In some settings, orcs and humans may
```
occasionally interbreed, resulting in half-orcs. They
are almost as hardy as orcs, but not so dimwitted.
We decide they will have a +3 Constitution stat bo-
nus, -3 Empathy, -1 Reasoning, -2 Self-Discipline,
and +2 Strength. The Superior Stat and Inferior Stat
talents cost 4 DP per bonus or -4 DP per penalty, so
we can add up the total stat modifiers, getting a net
-1 for a cost of -4 DP. We decide half-orcs will have
```
+5 on their Physical RRs (the Physical Resistance
```
```
talent for 5 DP), half that of orcs. They also have
```
```
the Fast Healer talent just like orcs (10 DP) and
```
```
Nightvision (10 DP), but lack Darkvision. The total
```
```
cost is -4 (Stats) + 5 (Physical Resistance) + 10
```
```
(Fast Healer) + 10 Nightvision for a total of 21 DP.
```
Subtracting that from 50, we get 29 bonus DP for
half-orc characters.
12.6. Creating
Cultures
The provided cultures are written very broadly to be
used in any fantasy setting. Many GMs will want to
create cultures that are more specific to their game
worlds or may, in some cases, even allow a player to
make a custom culture for his character. Keep in mind
that the skill ranks from a culture are those “anyone”
```
in the culture would have (i.e., anyone lacking this
```
```
skill set would be regarded as the village idiot). It does
```
```
not imply any special talent for the skills given (that
```
```
is the job of talents), although it may seem that way
```
to outsiders. A culture of nomads that take two ranks
of Riding every level will seem “born to the saddle.”
Cultures are created with a maximum of 80 DP using
```
costs given in the cost column of Table 2-3 (e.g., every
```
rank of Riding costs 2 DP, even if multiple ranks are
```
included in the culture).
```
```
Example: Building a culture with 7 ranks in “Other
```
Lores” costs 7 DP.
Created cultures should adhere to the following guide-
lines, especially if the GM is allowing a player to cre-
ate a culture. Some unique cultures may diverge from
these, but that should be strictly up to the GM.
•5-7 ranks in a native language. If literacy is com-
mon, add 3-6. Bilingual cultures may double these
values.
```
•5 ranks in Region Lore: Own Region (5 DP).
```
```
•1 rank in Body Development (3 DP)
```
```
•1 rank in Movement: Running (2 DP), or appropriate
```
```
Movement skill. (Swimming for mermen, Flying
```
```
for bird-men, etc.)
```
```
•1 rank in Combat Training: Unarmed (4 DP).
```
•There should be no ranks in Spell Lists.
•Skills may be assigned to a general skill such as
“Crafting” left to the player to specify, or to a
more specific skill such as “Woodcraft”.
Small pocket cultures are more common than new
races, but culture creation is still an element of world
creation that GMs may want to keep to themselves.
Seek permission from the GM before creating a new
culture.
langUages
Language can be a unifying element among groups
with varying racial or cultural backgrounds. On the
other hand, it can also be a barrier that can lead to
the destruction of a hearty group of adventurers. Be-
cause most worlds embrace a number of tongues and
few characters know all the languages, translators and
cooperative efforts may be necessary to overcome
communication problems. By having each player’s
```
character know and/or understand (to varying degrees)
```
different languages, a tremendous amount of diversity
can be injected into the game.
To allow for games that place emphasis on languages
with multi-lingual cultures, allow starting characters
```
to take the multilingual talent (Section 4.7).
```
12.7. Creating
Professions
New professions can be easily created by using one
of the existing professions as a template. Professions
are created using a point system, called aptitudes. Ap-
titudes are spent to raise and lower the cost of skill
categories, and can be used to gain access to spell lists
and realms.
DP costs are adjusted on a scale of fixed steps, thus
increasing the DP cost of a skill category by one step
250
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster Law
causes it to go to the next higher DP cost. The skill
costs are: 1/2, 1/3, 2/3, 2/4, 3/4, 3/5, 4/6, 5/7, 6/8, 7/10,
9/12, 12/15, and 15/20. Skills cannot be lowered be-
low a cost of 1/2, or raised above 15/20. The GM is
advised to use the existing professions as guidelines
for best and worst skill costs. No one is better at fight-
ing than a fighter or better at magic than the existing
```
pure spellcasters; conversely, no one is worse at magic
```
than a fighter or worse at fighting than a lay healer or
illusionist.
Reducing the cost of a skill category by one step costs
```
aptitude(s), and increasing the cost of a skill category
```
```
by one step gives aptitude(s) back. Most categories are
```
1 aptitude each except for the Spellcasting and Combat
Training categories, which are 3 aptitudes each. Any
change to Combat Training affects all of the Combat
Training costs, they are not adjusted independently,
and likewise for Spellcasting.
```
Example: Reducing the cost of Spellcasting by 1 step
```
requires raising the cost of other categories by a
```
total of 3 steps (e.g., one category by 3 steps, three
```
```
categories by 1 step). All of the Spellcasting skills
```
```
(Base, Open, Closed, Restricted, Arcane, Magical
```
```
Ritual) are all reduced by 1 step.
```
After assigning DP costs, each profession must be as-
signed 15 possible professional skills. This is the list
from which characters may select professional skills
and knacks.
```
Finally, spellcasting professions have a realm (or
```
```
realms, if a hybrid), and 6 base spell lists; each of the
```
existing spellcasting and hybrid professions have al-
ready had the aptitude cost applied for access to these
lists. Base Lists cost 2 aptitudes for every 3 base lists,
and each additional realm costs 5 aptitudes. Hybrid
casters also receive a two step discount on Arcane
spell lists, reflecting their better understanding of how
to combine magic from multiple realms.
Custom-built professions to fit a game world are the
GM’s domain. If a player wants to create a custom
profession, he should seek GM clearance that such a
thing is even allowed before proceeding.
12.8. Skills
The skill list provided is intended to be a default list,
generally applicable to most fantasy campaigns. Most
Gamemasters, or players, will wish to add skills or
specializations to help flesh out the character, such
as specific Lore or Crafting skills. Different settings
or genres may have unique technology or magic that
will require adding skills. Whenever possible, it is
preferable to add specializations, which can benefit
from existing professional skill bonuses, rather than
entirely new skills. Similarly, some skills may not be
appropriate for all settings. In particular, the Adrenal
```
skills (in the Body Discipline category) can allow for
```
near superhuman athletic feats. Using them can help
keep Arms Users on par with spellcasters, however
some GMs may not want superhuman feats to be pos-
sible without magic.
If the number of skills is changed drastically, such as
by removing a lot of specializations and only requir-
ing development in the more general skill, the number
of Development Points gained each level should be
adjusted. There is no hard and fast rule, as it depends
on how important the skills are that were added or
removed. As a starting point, consider adding or sub-
tracting 1 DP for every 2 skills.
12.9. Talents & Flaws
Talents and flaws should be considered completely
optional, where the GM has final say if each talent
and flaw is available or not. Talents and combinations
of talents can often upset game balance, so some care
should be taken when allowing them, with the default
being no. Some suggestions for using talents/flaws in
your game are:
•Allow talents, but only the bonus DP due to race
may be spent on a set of initial talents at character
creation. No new talents may be gained afterward,
although the initial talents may be improved upon.
•Characters have unrestricted access to talents at
character creation but only with points gained via
flaws.
•Elves are particularly magical in this setting, so
Elves may take up to 30 DP in all “Magical” tal-
ents, while other talent purchases are limited to 20
DP.
•A GM knows his group has trouble staying on track
and would like to see less digression in the next
campaign, so he cuts the cost of Destiny Sense in
half.
•The GM has players write up character backgrounds
first, then decides appropriate talents that would
fit those backgrounds to offer to players. They can
choose among the offered talents, but are not re-
quired to take any.
•Discipline talents are restricted to only two monastic
orders that teach the secrets to only full members
of the order, and are apt to hunt down and punish
those who break the secrecy of the order.
251
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster LawChapter 13:Chapter 13:
HealthHealth
252
ROLEMASTER
UNIFIED

Gamemaster Law
The health, life, and death of characters, allies, and en-
emies, play an important role in a campaign. Health is
most often adversely affected by injuries, but it could
also be affected by short term fatigue effects.
13.1. Healing
In a game where combat and dangerous maneuvers are
frequent, healing becomes an important part of game-
play. In Rolemaster, where characters can bleed, break
bones and lose limbs, healing becomes a detailed part
of gameplay where specif-
ic injuries require specific
healing methods, be it
from skills or spells.
Concussion hits are an
abstract way of tracking
a character’s pain, degree
of injuries and hold on
consciousness. A charac-
ter reduced to 0 or fewer
hits will fall unconscious.
Characters at less than full
hits will feel bruised, beat-
en and shaken.
Even without spells, herbs
or medicine, a combatant
may rally from some in-
juries, barring infections.
Hits are recovered over
time, from rest or sleep.
For every two continuous
hours of rest or sleep, 10%
of the character’s total hits
are recovered.
```
Injuries (specific damage
```
```
to a part of the body) apply
```
a penalty that represents
the pain and incapacity
caused by the injury. They
require longer recovery
even after being ‘healed.’
Injuries are assigned a severity depending on the pen-
```
alty (or amount of bleeding) as shown in Table 13-1a.
```
The injury severity is used in spell descriptions and for
healing maneuvers.
Injuries may be treated using Medicine skill, healing
herbs, or spells. The use of Medicine skill is described
in the sections below along with treatment options and
difficulties. Use of healing herbs requires an Herb-
alism maneuver which, if successful, grants +25 on
the Medicine maneuver to treat the injury. Mundane
healing herbs will not provide any advantage beyond
the bonus to the Medicine maneuver, but appropriate
magical herbs provide additional advantages as shown
on Table 13-1a and discussed below. Healing herbs are
described in Treasure Law. Healing spells are effec-
tive without the need for a Medicine maneuver. These
spells are described in Spell Law.
When an injury is treated, a d100OE recovery roll
```
is made (Table 13-1a), adding the character’s Body
```
Development ranks, their Constitution stat bonus,
and any listed modifier
for the type of healing
```
(medicine, magic, and/or
```
```
herbalism), and subtract-
```
ing the injury penalty for
the injury to determine
how long it will take to
recover. The result is the
number of days it takes
for a light injury penalty
to completely fade. Me-
dium and severe injuries
multiply the days by x5
and x10. Some races also
have a recovery multiplier
```
other than 1 (x1/2 or x2),
```
which further multiplies
the recovery time. With
proper facilities, each day
of complete and utter rest
is treated as two days. The
modifier for Medicine
Success indicates a suc-
cessful Medicine maneu-
```
ver to treat the injury (with
```
difficulties according to
```
the treatment type), while
```
Magic indicates the use
of a healing or relief spell
```
appropriate to the injury;
```
the two modifiers are not
cumulative. Herbalism
Success indicates a successful Medicine maneuver to
treat the injury while successfully using an appropri-
ate magical healing herb.
Divide the penalty by the recovery time to determine
how much the penalty is reduced every day. If the re-
covery time is short and the penalty moderate, it may
make more sense to measure recovery by the hour. If
it does not divide evenly, reduce the penalty the most
```
on the first day(s) (so for a -15 penalty that heals in 10
```
13. Health13. Health
Table 13-1a: Injuries & Recovery
RollBoneCuts & BurnsMuscle/TendonOrganPoison/DiseaseSkin/Tissue
< 0 10 12 15 20 25 5
1 - 50 8 10 12 16 20 4

76 - 100 4 6 7 8 10 2

126-175 2 2 3 4 5 1
176+ 1 1 1 2 3 1
Medium injuries x5, Head injuries x2, Severe injuries x10
Injury Penalty Bleeding Severity

-21 to -40 4-6/rd Medium

Type of Healing Modifier
